message.content: read the changes on this branch and get a comprehensive understanding of it. the previous copy was centered on non-technical folk and on junior devs while the current one focuses on more senior devs. however, research ai hero and the claude code for real engineers live cohort, in order to better differentiate my product from theirs. i want to talk about ai as well and about claude specifically. misconceptions are a differentiator, but that's more the underlying theme, not a marketing thing. i need a string position, a clear message, and the right angle.

content.text: I'll read the branch changes and research the competitors in parallel.

input.description: Show the main page diff
Show FAQ and footer changes
Research AI Hero product
input.prompt:
Research "AI Hero" (aihero.dev or similar) - it's a product/course/platform related to AI engineering education. I need to understand:
1. What exactly is AI Hero? (product, course, community?)
2. Who is it for? (target audience)
3. What's their core value proposition / positioning?
4. What do they teach/offer?
5. How do they talk about themselves? (messaging, tone)
6. Pricing model

Search the web thoroughly and visit their website to get accurate information. Return a comprehensive summary.

content.content:
diff --git a/apps/web/app/[locale]/components/faqs.tsx b/apps/web/app/[locale]/components/faqs.tsx index f1b70c9..ed1263c 100644 --- a/apps/web/app/[locale]/components/faqs.tsx +++ b/apps/web/app/[locale]/components/faqs.tsx @@ -3,44 +3,29 @@ import { Heading, Subheading } from "./text"; const courseFrequentlyAskedQuestions = [ { - question: "Who is this for?", + question: "Do I need to know how to code already?", answer: - "hasToggle is for anyone ready to dive into web development, whether you\u2019re just starting out or already a junior developer looking to level up your skills. If you\u2019re non-technical and want to break into coding, we\u2019ll help you build a strong foundation. If you\u2019ve already started your journey, welcome\u2014there\u2019s plenty here to help you grow.", + "No, but you\u2019ll learn how software works so you can direct AI effectively. hasToggle starts from the fundamentals and builds up to production-level web apps. The goal isn\u2019t to memorize syntax \u2014 it\u2019s to understand how things fit together so you can orchestrate AI to build what you envision.", }, { - question: "What if I already know some programming?", + question: "Is this just another prompt engineering course?", answer: - "That\u2019s awesome! Prior programming experience will give you an edge as you dive into web development. Your existing knowledge will help you grasp concepts more deeply, and you\u2019ll find new ways to think about coding while refining your skills and building exciting projects.", + "No. You learn to build real web apps by orchestrating AI agents, not just writing prompts. hasToggle teaches architecture, debugging, code review, and shipping \u2014 the skills that matter when AI is doing the typing.", }, { - question: "Are we going to build real-world web apps?", + question: "What if AI gets it wrong?", answer: - "Absolutely! We\u2019ll start by building static sites with just HTML and CSS. Then, we\u2019ll layer in JavaScript to make them dynamic. By the end, you\u2019ll use modern tools like React and Next.js to create fully functional web applications that reflect real-world use cases.", + "That\u2019s exactly why you need to understand the fundamentals. AI is powerful but not infallible. We teach you to review, debug, and guide AI so you catch mistakes before they ship. Understanding how code works is what separates builders from prompt-typists.", }, { - question: "Is this about frontend or backend development?", + question: "What tech stack will I learn?", answer: - "Both! This course covers fullstack development. We begin with frontend basics like HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Then, we dive into React.js and rebuild our apps with modern tools. Later, we add backend functionality using Node.js and transition to Next.js, a framework for building fullstack web applications.", + "Next.js, React, TypeScript, and modern AI tools \u2014 the same stack used to build hasToggle itself. You\u2019ll learn fullstack web development with the tools that professional teams actually use in production.", }, { - question: "What will I learn in this course?", + question: "What\u2019s in the weekly digest?", answer: - "This course has over 40 carefully crafted modules, covering everything from the fundamentals of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript to advanced topics like React, Next.js, and fullstack application development.", - }, - { - question: "Are we going to use AI?", - answer: - "Yes! Once we\u2019ve covered the basics, you\u2019ll learn how to use AI as a coding assistant. We\u2019ll explore how AI can generate code, debug problems, and help you when you\u2019re stuck. But don\u2019t worry \u2014 we focus on understanding the fundamentals first because AI works best when you know what to ask for.", - }, - { - question: "Do I have to do any exercises?", - answer: - "It\u2019s up to you! hasToggle is designed to adapt to your preferred level of engagement. Whether you prefer to simply watch the videos and follow along, or get hands-on with interactive exercises for a deeper dive, the choice is yours. input.prompt:
Research "Claude Code for Real Engineers" - it appears to be a live cohort course or program. I need to understand:
1. What exactly is it? (course, cohort, community?)
2. Who runs it?
3. Who is it for? (target audience)
4. What's their core value proposition / positioning?
5. What do they teach?
6. How do they talk about themselves? (messaging, tone)
7. Pricing model

Search the web thoroughly and visit their website to get accurate information. Return a comprehensive summary.

content.text:
Here is a comprehensive summary of "Claude Code for Real Engineers":

---

## 1. What Is It?

A **2-week, cohort-based online course** run through the AI Hero platform (aihero.dev). It consists of 8 workshop modules delivered asynchronously (weekly content drops each Monday), supplemented by 6 live office hours across 3 dates, staggered for multiple time zones. There is also a private Discord community for each cohort. It is not an ongoing community or subscription -- it is a defined 2-week program with lifetime access to materials afterward.

The next cohort runs **March 30 -- April 13, 2026**.

## 2. Who Runs It?

**Matt Pocock** -- widely known in the TypeScript community as the creator of **Total TypeScript** (described as "the industry standard course for learning TS"). He was previously a member of the XState core team and a developer advocate at Vercel. AI Hero is his newer platform, pivoting from TypeScript education into AI engineering education.

## 3. Target Audience

Experienced, professional software engineers who:
- Already write production code and have existing codebases
- Want to use AI coding assistants (specifically Claude Code CLI) in a disciplined, professional way
- Are concerned about code quality, technical debt, and maintainability
- Want to go beyond "vibe coding" / demo-level usage toward real production workflows

It is explicitly **not** for beginners or non-engineers. The Scrimba comparison article positions it as the premium option for "senior developers wanting depth and mentorship."

## 4. Core Value Proposition / Positioning

The central thesis is: **AI tools amplify your existing engineering skills -- they don't replace them.** The course positions itself against two failure modes:

1. **"Delegating everything"** to AI -- leads to spaghetti code and technical debt at speed
2. **"Delegating nothing"** -- creates bottlenecks and developer burnout

Key positioning line from the sales page: *"The better the tools get, the wider the gulf gets between vibe coders and real engineers."*

Matt's X/Twitter framing: *"Everyone thinks AI is a paradigm shift. That everything we've learned about building software in the last 20 years is for boomers. I disagree."*

The course argues that timeless engineering fundamentals (communication, planning, decomposition, systematization) become **more** important with AI, not less.

## 5. What They Teach (Curriculum)

**Week 1: Foundations, Steering & Planning**
- LLM constraints and context window management
- Plan/Execute/Clear workflow loops
- Steering via `CLAUDE.md` files and custom skills
- Progressive disclosure techniques
- PRD writing for feature decomposition
- "Tracer bullet" architecture validation
- Multi-phase planning for large features

**Week 2: Feedback Loops, Autonomy & Real-World Patterns**
- CI/CD pipeline integration for agent-written code quality
- Test strategy for agent workflows
- "Ralph Wiggum" loop pattern for autonomous execution (their branded term for AFK agent loops)
- Human-in-the-loop vs. fully autonomous modes
- GitHub Issues integration and kanban workflows
- Research phases for reducing rework
- Codebase preparation for agent navigation

## 6. How They Talk About Themselves (Messaging & Tone)

**Tone:** Direct, confident, slightly irreverent. Anti-hype but pro-AI. Engineer-to-engineer, not marketer-to-consumer. Uses concrete examples and strong opinions. Phrases like "vibe coders" are used dismissively. Warns that naive AI use will "leak valuable IP, embarrass companies, and piss off customers."

**Framing device:** "Vibe Coder vs. AI Hero" before/after comparison table on the sales page (e.g., "YOLO mode" vs. "Sandboxing and permissions," "Unmaintainable codebases" vs. "Well-structured modules").

**Social proof:** Andrej Karpathy quote on the page. Matt cites shipping 1,000+ commits and resolving 500+ issues using Claude Code with his autonomous loop patterns. The course is described as "the best course I've ever built."

**Brand identity:** "AI Hero" -- the aspirational identity for the student. Tagline: "AI Engineering for Curious Professional Developers."

## 7. Pricing

- **$795** per seat (standard price)
- Periodic launch discounts available
- 30-day money-back guarantee
- Invoices, team seats, and PPP (purchasing power parity) pricing available
- Note: students also need a Claude Pro subscription ($20/month minimum; Max 5x at $100/month recommended for autonomous sessions)

---

**Sources:**
- [Claude Code for Real Engineers - Course Page](https://www.aihero.dev/cohorts/claude-code-for-real-engineers-2026-04)
- [AI Hero Homepage](https://www.aihero.dev/)
- [Matt Pocock on X/Twitter](https://x.com/mattpocockuk/status/2033574421563130048)
- [Scrimba - Best Claude Code Tutorials and Courses in 2026](https://scrimba.com/articles/best-claude-code-tutorials-and-courses-in-2026/)
- [AI Hero GitHub](https://github.com/ai-hero-dev)

Here is a comprehensive summary of AI Hero:

---

## 1. What Exactly Is AI Hero?

AI Hero is an **AI engineering education platform** created by **Matt Pocock**, who is best known for creating Total TypeScript (widely considered the industry-standard TypeScript course). It launched as a natural evolution of his teaching career, pivoting from TypeScript education to AI engineering education.

The platform consists of:
- **Cohort-based paid courses** (the flagship product)
- **Free blog posts / articles** on AI engineering topics
- **A newsletter** for updates, tips, and announcements
- **An open-source GitHub repo** (`ai-hero-dev/ai-hero`) with runnable examples, exercises, and course material (1.4k stars, 261 forks)
- **A private Discord community** for paid students

The tagline is **"From Zero to AI Hero."**

## 2. Target Audience

Professional software developers -- specifically frontend, backend, and full-stack engineers -- who want to stay relevant as AI reshapes software engineering. The messaging explicitly targets people caught between two emotions:
- **Fear**: "Will I still be doing this in 5 years?"
- **Hype**: The temptation to think everything they know about building software is obsolete

AI Hero positions itself as the practical middle ground for developers who want to use AI tools with engineering discipline, not abandon it.

They claim **54,000+ developers** are in their learning community.

## 3. Core Value Proposition / Positioning

The central thesis is: **"Real engineering is the only way to generate safe, worthwhile, long-lasting code -- by hand, or with AI tools."**

Key positioning points:
- AI coding tools are powerful but dangerous without engineering fundamentals
- There are two failure modes: delegating everything to AI (leading to technical debt) or delegating nothing (causing burnout and irrelevance)
- The course teaches developers to **delegate confidently** to AI agents while maintaining quality
- Explicitly anti-"vibe coding" -- they distinguish between throwaway demos and production-grade systems
- Quote from their messaging: **"The world still belongs to the builders"**

## 4. What They Teach / Offer

**Current flagship course: "Claude Code for Real Engineers"**
- 2-week async cohort (the current one runs March 30 - April 13, 2026)
- Focused specifically on using Claude Code in production workflows

**Curriculum covers:**
- LLM constraints and context window management
- Plan/Execute/Clear loop patterns
- Writing CLAUDE.md files and custom skills
- PRD writing and feature decomposition
- "Tracer bullets" for architecture validation
- CI/CD integration and test strategies
- Human-in-the-loop vs. fully autonomous modes
- Research phases and kanban workflows
- Codebase preparation for AI navigation

**Broader AI engineering topics (from articles/repo):**
- Prompt engineering, structured outputs
- RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation)
- Agents and agent workflows
- Evals and observability (they built an open-source eval framework called Evalite)

**Format details:**
- Async lessons drop on Mondays
- 6 live Office Hours across 3 days with timezone staggering
- Lifetime access to lessons, exercises, code repo, recordings, and transcripts
- Private Discord community access

## 5. How They Talk About Themselves (Messaging & Tone)

The tone is:
- **Confident and opinionated** -- takes a clear stance against "vibe coding"
- **Empathetic to developer anxiety** -- acknowledges the fear around AI disruption without being dismissive
- **Practical and engineering-focused** -- avoids hype, emphasizes fundamentals and production-readiness
- **Builder identity** -- positions AI as a tool for craftspeople, not a replacement for them
- **Credibility-driven** -- leans heavily on Matt Pocock's reputation from Total TypeScript and endorsements from figures like Guillermo Rauch (Vercel CEO) and Andrej Karpathy

The messaging reads like a senior engineer talking to peers, not a marketer selling a product. It's direct, uses concrete technical language, and appeals to professional pride.

## 6. Pricing Model

- The "Claude Code for Real Engineers" cohort is priced at approximately **$795 at full price**
- Launch-week discount of **40% off** was offered
- **30-day refund policy**
- **Purchase Power Parity (PPP)** support for international buyers
- **Team seat options** available
- Requires a separate Claude Pro subscription ($20/month minimum) or Claude Max 5x ($100/month)

The pricing model follows the same pattern as Total TypeScript: premium-priced cohort courses with lifetime access, targeting professional developers who can expense it or justify the investment through career growth.

---

Sources:
- [AI Hero Homepage](https://www.aihero.dev/)
- [Claude Code for Real Engineers Cohort](https://www.aihero.dev/cohorts/claude-code-for-real-engineers-2026-04)
- [AI Hero GitHub Repository](https://github.com/ai-hero-dev/ai-hero)
- [AI Hero on EveryDev.ai](https://www.everydev.ai/tools/aihero)
- [Matt Pocock on X about the course](https://x.com/mattpocockuk/status/2033574421563130048)
- [AI Hero Newsletter](https://www.aihero.dev/newsletter)
- [Matt Pocock's AI Engineer Roadmap on LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/REDACTED)

Now let me also read the misconception wrapper and the metadata changes to complete my picture.

Okay, I've now got a full picture of the branch changes and the competitive landscape. Here's my analysis:

---

## What's on this branch

The rewrite strips away the beginner/squirrel-themed landing page (Hazel mascot, confetti, insider jokes, curriculum bento grid, testimonials) and replaces it with a leaner page built around:

1. **Hero**: "Build more. Type less." — learn to build by directing AI, not memorizing syntax
2. **Misconception demos**: Interactive proof-points framed as "Misconception → Reality" (React state, optimistic updates, "just a button")
3. **About**: "AI amplifies. It doesn't replace."
4. **Digest CTA**: Weekly misconception-buster newsletter
5. **Streamlined FAQ**: Reframed around AI-empowered development

The positioning shift is clear: from "learn to code from scratch" to "learn to build production apps by orchestrating AI."

---

## The competitive problem

**AI Hero / Claude Code for Real Engineers** (Matt Pocock, $795/cohort) occupies almost exactly the space you're moving toward:

- **Their thesis**: "AI tools amplify engineering skills, they don't replace them." Your About section literally says: "AI amplifies. It doesn't replace."
- **Their audience**: Senior/experienced devs who want to use AI professionally
- **Their angle**: Anti-vibe-coding, pro-fundamentals, engineering discipline with AI
- **Their format**: Cohort courses, async content, Discord community
- **Their credibility**: Matt Pocock (Total TypeScript), Vercel alumni, Karpathy endorsement

If you shift hasToggle to target senior devs who want to use AI for production work, you're competing head-on with someone who has massive distribution and credibility in exactly that niche.

---

## Where the gap is

AI Hero teaches **how to use Claude Code** — the workflow, the prompts, the CLAUDE.md files, the Plan/Execute/Clear loop. It's a course about **operating the tool**.

What neither AI Hero nor any course I found teaches is **what you need to know about the things you're building** in order to direct AI well. There's an assumption that their audience already has deep engineering knowledge. But the reality is:

1. Many "senior" devs have gaps they've papered over with Stack Overflow / AI
2. The misconceptions that trip people up aren't about the AI tool — they're about **the domain** (React rendering, optimistic updates, auth flows, database modeling)
3. AI makes those gaps more dangerous, not less, because you ship faster without understanding

**This is your angle. AI Hero teaches you how to drive the car. hasToggle teaches you how the engine works — so you know where to tell the car to go.**

---

## Proposed positioning

**One-liner**: "Know what you're building, not just how to prompt."

**The thesis**: The bottleneck in AI-assisted development isn't the AI tool — it's your mental model of the system you're building. If you don't understand React re-renders, you can't review the code Claude writes. If you don't understand optimistic updates, you won't know to ask for them. hasToggle builds the engineering intuition that makes AI actually useful.

**Who it's for**: Developers who use AI daily but keep hitting walls they can't explain. They can get code out of Claude, but they can't tell if it's right. They ship features but can't debug them when they break. They're not beginners — they're **capable devs with blind spots**.

**What makes it different from AI Hero**:

| | AI Hero | hasToggle |
|---|---|---|
| Teaches | How to operate Claude Code | How web apps actually work |
| Format | 2-week cohort, $795 | Weekly content, accessible |
| Assumes | You already have deep engineering knowledge | You have gaps — everyone does |
| Outcome | Better AI workflows | Better engineering judgment |
| Metaphor | Driving school for a new car | Mechanical knowledge so you know what the car should do |

**The misconception framing is your weapon** — but not as a marketing gimmick. It's the pedagogical model. Every piece of content surfaces a specific gap in understanding that AI makes dangerous. That's genuinely unique. AI Hero doesn't do this. Nobody does this.

---

## What I'd change on the current page

The current copy is halfway there but still hedging. Some specific issues:

1. **"Build more. Type less."** — This could be an AI Hero tagline. It's about productivity, not understanding. Consider something like: **"Know what you're building."** or **"AI writes the code. You need to know if it's right."**

2. **"AI amplifies. It doesn't replace."** — This is literally AI Hero's thesis. Drop it or reframe it around *your* unique angle (mental models, not workflow).

3. **"Learn to build production web apps by directing AI"** — "Directing AI" is AI Hero's entire curriculum. Your differentiator is the *understanding* underneath. Consider: "Learn how production web apps actually work — so AI can build them for you."

4. **The misconception demos are the strongest thing on the page** — they should be more prominent, more numerous, and the entire brand identity. Not just a section — the structural principle.

5. **"Created by Eric — developer, educator, and believer in AI-empowered individuals"** — Too generic. What's your specific credibility? What have you shipped? What have you seen break?

---

## The sharp version

> **hasToggle — Know what you're building.**
> 
> AI writes the code. But it can't tell you if it's right. hasToggle builds the engineering intuition you need to review, debug, and ship AI-generated code with confidence. One misconception at a time.

This positions you as complementary to (not competing with) AI Hero. Someone could take both. AI Hero teaches the Claude workflow. hasToggle teaches the web development knowledge that makes that workflow produce good software.

Want me to draft revised copy for the landing page sections?

message.content:
agree with 4. The misconception demos are the strongest thing on the page — they should be more prominent, more numerous, and the entire brand identity. Not just a section — the structural principle.

everything else i do not agree with to be honest. build more, type less, is not a good tagline. let's brainstorm this.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/eric/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.0.6/skills/brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer visual companion** (if topic will involve visual questions) — this is its own message, not combined with a clarifying question. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec self-review** — quick inline check for placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope (see below)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Visual questions ahead?" [shape=diamond];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" [shape=box];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Visual questions ahead?";
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [label="yes"];
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Ask clarifying questions" [label="no"];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)";
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" -> "User reviews spec?";
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Self-Review:**
After writing the spec document, look at it with fresh eyes:

1. **Placeholder scan:** Any "TBD", "TODO", incomplete sections, or vague requirements? Fix them.
2. **Internal consistency:** Do any sections contradict each other? Does the architecture match the feature descriptions?
3. **Scope check:** Is this focused enough for a single implementation plan, or does it need decomposition?
4. **Ambiguity check:** Could any requirement be interpreted two different ways? If so, pick one and make it explicit.

Fix any issues inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on.

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion:** When you anticipate that upcoming questions will involve visual content (mockups, layouts, diagrams), offer it once for consent:
> "Some of what we're working on might be easier to explain if I can show it to you in a web browser. I can put together mockups, diagrams, comparisons, and other visuals as we go. This feature is still new and can be token-intensive. Want to try it? (Requires opening a local URL)"

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Do not combine it with clarifying questions, context summaries, or any other content. The message should contain ONLY the offer above and nothing else. Wait for the user's response before continuing. If they decline, proceed with text-only brainstorming.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`
Using the brainstorming skill to work through positioning.

content.text:
Alright, here's what I've gathered. Let me synthesize and propose three positioning approaches.

---

## What we know

- **Audience**: Working developers who want to level up
- **Vision**: Eventually broaden to non-technical people
- **Core belief**: AI will automate engineering discipline too — what remains is your ability to think, steer, and judge
- **Differentiator vs AI Hero**: They bet on "engineering discipline is timeless." You bet on "engineering discipline is next to be automated — what's left is you"
- **Pedagogical model**: Misconceptions as the structural principle; meta-skills (checking assumptions, questioning from valid premises, clear thinking)
- **AI role**: Teach with Claude specifically, but lessons transfer to any AI. AI is also the training ground for developing meta-skills
- **Format**: Free content (digest, demos) builds audience → paid live cohort
- **Monetization**: Similar range to AI Hero (~$795)

---

## Three approaches

### Approach 1: "Think for yourself"

**Position**: hasToggle teaches developers the thinking skills that AI can't replace — yet. As AI gets smarter, the gap between people who can steer it and people who can't will be the only gap that matters.

**Tone**: Provocative, philosophical, slightly contrarian. Makes people feel like they're seeing something others haven't.

**Tagline direction**: Something like *"The last skill AI won't learn for you"* or *"Think for yourself. Build with AI."*

**Strengths**: Sharp differentiation. Thought-leadership angle. Ages well as AI improves.
**Weaknesses**: Could feel abstract. "Think better" is hard to sell concretely. Risk of sounding preachy.

---

### Approach 2: "AI is only as good as you are"

**Position**: You're already using AI to build. The question is whether you actually understand what it's building. hasToggle develops the judgment to direct AI effectively — through misconception-busting content that reveals what you don't know you don't know.

**Tone**: Direct, practical, slightly confrontational. Challenges the reader's confidence without insulting them.

**Tagline direction**: Something like *"AI is only as good as you are"* or *"You're the bottleneck, not the AI"*

**Strengths**: Immediately resonant for anyone who's shipped AI-generated code and felt uneasy. Concrete — ties directly to the misconception demos. Creates urgency.
**Weaknesses**: Could alienate people who feel attacked. Less visionary, more present-tense.

---

### Approach 3: "Become the engineer AI needs you to be"

**Position**: AI changes what it means to be an engineer. The typing is over. What's left is architecture, judgment, review, and the meta-cognitive skills to know when something is wrong. hasToggle trains you for that new role.

**Tone**: Aspirational, forward-looking. Positions the reader as evolving into something better, not fixing a deficiency.

**Tagline direction**: Something like *"The new engineering"* or *"What's left when AI writes the code"*

**Strengths**: Aspirational — people buy into becoming something, not being told they're lacking. Future-proof framing. Works for both current devs and eventual non-technical audience.
**Weaknesses**: "New engineering" could feel buzzwordy. Less sharp than Approach 2. Could blend in with generic AI education messaging.

---

## My recommendation

**Approach 2**, with elements of 1's edge. Here's why:

- The misconception format is inherently confrontational in a good way — "here's what you think, here's why you're wrong, here's the truth." The positioning should match that energy.
- "AI is only as good as you are" is concrete, testable (the demos *prove it*), and creates a natural funnel: free content shows you your gaps → paid cohort fills them.
- It avoids the abstract "think better" framing of Approach 1 while still carrying the meta-skills thesis underneath.
- It differentiates cleanly from AI Hero: they say "engineering discipline matters." You say "yes, and you probably have less of it than you think — let's fix that."

Approach 3 is the safest but least memorable. Approach 1 is the most interesting intellectually but hardest to sell.

**What resonates? Want me to develop one of these, combine elements, or am I still off?**

message.content:
I like that: The misconception format is inherently confrontational in a good way — "here's what you think, here's why you're wrong, here's the truth." The positioning should match that energy.

I like the positive energy of Approach 3. We should keep that in our messaging. And we should apply the confrontational bit to demos where misconceptions are revealing what we're not knowing, in a good way.

I think there's place for both, positive high energy in messaging and a confrontational style in demos and exercises.

For instance, a developer builds a system with ai but the system's default behavior is to delete db records when the system encounters errors. that's a wrong default. ai still complied with every instruction of the developer.

content.text:
That example is perfect — it crystallizes everything. AI did exactly what it was told. The failure was the human's mental model of what "handle errors" should mean. That's a misconception demo that *stings* in the right way.

So the shape is:

- **Messaging/positioning**: Aspirational, positive energy — you're becoming a better engineer, AI is your ally, this is exciting
- **Demos/exercises**: Confrontational reveals — "you thought this was fine, here's why it's not, here's what you didn't know to ask for"

The demos earn the trust that the positive messaging promises. That's a strong dynamic.

This is sharpening. Let me check one thing before I propose the full design:

**The "what's left when AI writes the code" framing — does that land for you as the core question hasToggle answers?** Because that seems to tie everything together:

- The positive answer: *You* are what's left — your judgment, your ability to steer, your meta-skills. And those can be trained.
- The confrontational proof: Here's a demo where AI wrote perfect code that does the wrong thing, because the human didn't know what to ask for.
- The Claude angle: We use Claude to build, to teach, and to expose the gaps.
- The long-term vision: As AI gets smarter, this question only gets more important — not less.

Does this feel like the right central thread, or is there something missing?

message.content: feels right

content.text:
Let me present the design section by section. Tell me after each whether it's right.

---

## Section 1: Core positioning

**Central question**: What's left when AI writes the code?

**Answer**: You are. Your judgment, your ability to steer, your ability to know when something is wrong before it ships. hasToggle trains that.

**The thesis**: AI will keep getting smarter. It already writes code, and it's learning to reason. But the person directing it still needs to know what "right" looks like — not at the syntax level, but at the system level. What should the default behavior be? What failure modes exist? What did you forget to ask for? These are meta-skills: checking your own assumptions, questioning from valid premises, knowing what you don't know. hasToggle builds them.

**Tone duality**:
- Messaging and positioning: Aspirational, positive, forward-looking. "You're becoming the engineer this era needs."
- Demos and exercises: Confrontational reveals. "You thought this was fine. Here's why it's not."

**Relationship to AI Hero**: Complementary, not competitive. AI Hero teaches how to operate Claude Code professionally. hasToggle teaches the thinking underneath — the judgment that makes any tool effective. Someone could (and should) use both.

**Relationship to Claude**: Claude is the primary AI tool taught with. Content is built around working with Claude. But the meta-skills and lessons transfer to any AI. Claude is the vehicle, not the destination.

---

Does this section feel right so far?

see what else we can extract from the transcript and align with the ideas thus far.

content.text:
Okay, I've read the full transcript. This is Mo Gawdat (former Chief Business Officer at Google X) on Silicon Valley Girl. Here's what I extracted that aligns with and sharpens what we've been building:

---

**Key ideas from the transcript that connect to hasToggle:**

1. **"AI is going to make you dumb if you outsource your problem solving to AI. AI is going to make you the smartest you've ever been if you take the parts that are not natural to the human brain... get the AI to do the work so that you do the intelligence."** — This is your thesis in one quote. The distinction between outsourcing *thinking* vs outsourcing *work*.

2. **The calculator analogy**: Mo learned math without a calculator first, then used the calculator to do the solution *twice*. Most people used the time savings to leave early. This maps directly to your misconceptions approach — you teach people what's underneath so they can use AI to go further, not to skip the thinking.

3. **"Stop being gullible. Stop believing everything that you're taught."** — This is meta-cognition. Checking your assumptions, questioning from valid premises. Mo calls it the top skill in the world. You're teaching this through the lens of code.

4. **AI as extension of humanity, not replacement** — "You are now for the first time given an extra connection to extra memory, to an archive of all human memory and knowledge." Your angle isn't anti-AI. It's about becoming a better *human* who uses AI as amplification.

5. **"Education is over"** — The traditional technology of education (lectures, exams, degrees) is dead. But *learning* isn't. Someone has to teach people how to actually use AI to become smarter. Mo's exact question: "Who's going to teach our kids to do that?" — That's you.

---

**What this changes about the positioning:**

The transcript makes me think the framing shouldn't center on "what's left when AI writes the code" — that's defensive. It should center on **what becomes possible when you use AI to think better instead of think less**.

The misconception format does this perfectly. Each misconception is a moment where someone outsourced their thinking to AI. The reveal isn't "AI got it wrong" — it's "you stopped thinking, and here's what that cost you."

So the core belief people walk away with:

**"AI makes you more powerful — but only if you keep thinking."**

Coding is where you prove it. But the skill is universal. This is what you meant by "broader than coding."

Does this feel like the right thread to pull on for the full design?

content.text:
Right. So the core belief isn't just "keep thinking" — it's deeper than that. It's: **you have something AI doesn't. Values, ethics, judgment about what *should* exist. AI is power. You decide what it's for.**

The misconceptions then become moments where someone let AI decide the *what* when they should have only let it handle the *how*. The developer who let AI delete records on error didn't just have a technical gap — they abdicated a decision about how their system should behave.

Let me sharpen this into something punchy:

**"AI is power. Learn to use it well."**

Six words. "Power" captures the amplification (Mo's "borrowed 80 IQ points"). "Well" carries the ethics, the judgment, the meta-cognition — without being preachy about it. And "learn" is what hasToggle does.

Or a slightly different angle:

**"AI gives you power. What you do with it is up to you."**

This one makes the human responsibility explicit. But it might be too long for a tagline.

Or leaning into the extension-of-self framing:

**"Become the engineer AI needs you to be."** — but we already discussed this one and it's narrower than your vision.

Which direction feels strongest? Or should I keep pulling?

see what you can pull for the tagline and belief.

content.text:
Good distinction — tagline serves the current product (coding), belief system carries the broader vision underneath.

**Belief system** (what people absorb over time through your content):

*AI doesn't replace you. It reveals you.* It reveals what you understand, what you assumed, what you never questioned. And when you learn to use it well, it amplifies everything you are. The future belongs to people who use AI to become more — not less — human.

This is positive, empowering, not fear-based. It frames AI as a mirror and an amplifier. The misconception demos are the "reveals" — each one shows you something about your own thinking. The learning is the "amplifies" part.

**Tagline options** (narrow to coding, for now):

1. **"AI writes the code. You call the shots."** — Clear role separation. You're in charge. Positive energy.

2. **"Ship with AI. Think for yourself."** — Action-oriented. The two halves of what hasToggle teaches.

3. **"Code smarter. Not less."** — Riff on Mo's calculator insight. Direct.

4. **"You direct. AI delivers."** — Aspirational. Positions the developer as the one with authority.

5. **"Build with AI. Know what you're building."** — The gap hasToggle fills. Slightly confrontational but still positive.

6. **"Think clearly. Build with AI."** — Meta-skills first, AI second. Order matters.

My gut says **1** or **4** for tone — they're confident, positive, and put the human in the driver's seat. They also work as the visible tagline while the deeper belief system ("AI reveals and amplifies you") lives in the content.

What pulls you?

**Round 2:**

2. **"What you don't know will ship."** — Same energy, more direct threat. The misconception format is literally about revealing what you don't know.

3. **"The code runs. But is it right?"** — The core tension. AI gives you working code. hasToggle teaches you whether it's the *right* code.

4. **"AI builds what you ask for. Not what you need."** — Pins the gap on the human's understanding, not the AI's capability.

5. **"Know what you're asking for."** — Short. The meta-skill in five words. Works for coding now, works for everything later.

6. **"Learn what AI can't teach itself."** — Positions hasToggle as the thing between you and the machine. The judgment, the values, the "should we."

Different energy this time?

2

4	
5	The second rule of AI is: *you do not trust the output.*
6

Authoritative. He ships the fix without reading it twice. He doesn't read anything twice. He tells the team it's handled. He goes to lunch.
8	
9	Two hours later the production database is on fire and he's standing in the kitchen saying *"it made something up"* like the machine owed him something. Like they had a contract.
10	
11	I am not here to blame him. I am here to tell you what he is.
12	
13	He is you. He is all of us.
14	
15	The interfaces are designed this way. Clean text. Proper grammar. Numbered lists. The calm, measured tone of someone who went to a good school and reads a lot. It is the most natural thing in the world to mistake that fluency for understanding.
16	
17	*Sticking feathers up your butt does not make you a chicken.*
18

20	
21	A large language model is a completion engine. That's it. You give it a sequence of words and it predicts the next word. Then the next. Then the next. Not because it *knows* the answer. Because the answer is *statistically probable*. The training process — months of grinding through the sum total of human text — optimizes for one thing: what word comes next. Not what's true. Not what's right. What's *likely*.
22	
23	There is no fact-checking step. There is no lookup. There is no tiny librarian inside the GPU running to the stacks. Every single output is a statistical hallucination that happens, sometimes, to intersect with reality.
24	
25	You are not talking to someone who knows things. You are talking to a very sophisticated parrot that has eaten every book ever written and is now regurgitating word-soup that *sounds* like knowledge.
26

It carries on conversations that feel — and this is the dangerous part — *meaningful*. This is the hook. This is the moment where the con works. Because when the output is right, you think: this thing is smart. This thing understands.
28

30	
31	We have a word for when the machine gets it wrong. We call it *hallucination*. Like the system normally operates in some pristine state of truth and occasionally, regrettably, slips up. Oops. A glitch. An aberration.
32	
33	That framing is a lie.
34	
35	**Hallucination is not the failure mode. Hallucination is the default mode.**
36	
37	The miracle — the genuine, unexplainable miracle — is that a completion engine produces anything that *isn't* pure fiction. Accuracy is the accident. Accuracy is the emergent property. The base behavior is fabrication. What we call hallucination is just the base behavior becoming visible — the mask slipping — the model generating something plausible but wrong and you finally noticing.
38	
39	You met me at a very strange time in my life, the model would say, if it could say anything honest.
40	
41	So when you paste your question into that clean white text box and the response comes back — confident, formatted, authoritative — understand what just happened. Nobody looked anything up. file.content:
# You Do Not Trust the Output

The first rule of AI is: you do not trust the output.

The second rule of AI is: *you do not trust the output.*

I know this because I've watched it happen. Some guy — khakis, standup meeting energy, mass-produced confidence — pastes an error message into ChatGPT like he's feeding a dollar into a vending machine. Three paragraphs come back. Smooth. Authoritative. He ships the fix without reading it twice. He doesn't read anything twice. He tells the team it's handled. He goes to lunch.

Two hours later the production database is on fire and he's standing in the kitchen saying *"it made something up"* like the machine owed him something. Like they had a contract.

I am not here to blame him. I am here to tell you what he is.

He is you. He is all of us.

The interfaces are designed this way. Clean text. Proper grammar. Numbered lists. The calm, measured tone of someone who went to a good school and reads a lot. It is the most natural thing in the world to mistake that fluency for understanding.

*Sticking feathers up your butt does not make you a chicken.*

Here is what actually happens inside the machine. Pay attention, because this is the part nobody wants to hear.

A large language model is a completion engine. That's it. You give it a sequence of words and it predicts the next word. Then the next. Then the next. Not because it *knows* the answer. Because the answer is *statistically probable*. The training process — months of grinding through the sum total of human text — optimizes for one thing: what word comes next. Not what's true. Not what's right. What's *likely*.

There is no fact-checking step. There is no lookup. There is no tiny librarian inside the GPU running to the stacks. Every single output is a statistical hallucination that happens, sometimes, to intersect with reality.

You are not talking to someone who knows things. You are talking to a very sophisticated parrot that has eaten every book ever written and is now regurgitating word-soup that *sounds* like knowledge.

And here's the thing about the soup. The soup is *incredible*. It follows grammar. It writes working code. It solves math problems. It carries on conversations that feel — and this is the dangerous part — *meaningful*. This is the hook. This is the moment where the con works. Because when the output is right, you think: this thing is smart. This thing understands.

It doesn't understand anything.

We have a word for when the machine gets it wrong. We call it *hallucination*. Like the system normally operates in some pristine state of truth and occasionally, regrettably, slips up. Oops. A glitch. An aberration.

That framing is a lie.

**Hallucination is not the failure mode. Hallucination is the default mode.**

The miracle — the genuine, unexplainable miracle — is that a completion engine produces anything that *isn't* pure fiction. Accuracy is the accident. Accuracy is the emergent property. The base behavior is fabrication. What we call hallucination is just the base behavior becoming visible — the mask slipping — the model generating something plausible but wrong and you finally noticing.

You met me at a very strange time in my life, the model would say, if it could say anything honest.

So when you paste your question into that clean white text box and the response comes back — confident, formatted, authoritative — understand what just happened. Nobody looked anything up. Nobody checked. A probability engine produced the most statistically likely continuation of your prompt. Sometimes that continuation happens to be true. Sometimes it's a beautiful, convincing lie. The model cannot tell the difference.

And unless you verify it, neither can you.

This is where it gets interesting. This is the part after the basement fight, after the chemical burn, after you've let go of the idea that the machine is your friend.

You ground it.

You stop asking the model what it *knows* and you start telling it where to *look*. Documentation. Databases. APIs. Search results. Verified sources. You treat it like what it is — a reasoning engine, not a knowledge base. A tool for processing information, not a source of information. You put it on a leash.

The model doesn't become trustworthy. It becomes *useful*. There's a difference.

**That distinction changes everything about how you build with AI.**

You are not your LLM's output. You are not your chatbot's confidence score. You are not your AI's fluent bullshit.

Now stop nodding along and go verify something.

content.content:
1	# On the Subject of Artificial Thinking Engines, and Why They Don't
2	
3	It is a well-established fact* that humans will trust anything that sounds confident enough.
4	
5	*\*It is not, in fact, an established fact. But it sounded confident, which rather proves the point.*
6	
7	The Artificial Thinking Engine — or, as the people who built it preferred to call it, the Large Language Model, because naming things accurately would be bad for business — was the most impressive machine ever constructed for the purpose of sounding like it knew things. It did not, in any meaningful sense, know things. What it did was considerably more clever and considerably more dangerous: it *completed sentences*.
8	
9	You gave it the beginning of a sentence. It gave you the most statistically probable ending. That was the entire trick. The entire, multi-billion-dollar, reshaping-the-global-economy trick. A completion engine with very good taste in next words.
10	
11	"But it writes code!" said the engineers, who were very excited and had not yet been to production.
12	
13	"But it explains complex topics!" said the managers, who were even more excited and had never been to production.
14	
15	"But it sounds exactly like an expert!" said everyone, which was true in the same way that a parrot wearing a stethoscope sounds exactly like a doctor.**
16	
17	**\*The parrot, at least, is unlikely to prescribe the wrong medication with perfect confidence. The Thinking Engine had no such restraint.*
18	
19	The problem — and there was, inevitably, a problem, because there is always a problem when humans encounter something that tells them what they want to hear — was that the Engine was *equally confident when it was wrong*. It had to be. Confidence was not a feature it could adjust based on accuracy, because it had no access to accuracy. It had access to probability, which is related to accuracy in the same way that a map is related to the territory: usefully, but not reliably, and certainly not in the swamp bits.
20	
21	The humans called it "hallucination" when the Engine was wrong, because that sounded like a temporary medical condition rather than a permanent architectural feature.***
22	
23	***\*The word "hallucination" was chosen, one suspects, by the same type of person who calls a company-wide firing a "workforce optimization event." The Engine did not hallucinate. The Engine did exactly what it was designed to do. It completed sequences. Sometimes the sequences were true. Sometimes they weren't. The Engine could not tell the difference because the Engine did not have a concept of "difference." Or "true." Or "tell."*
24	
25	There was, however, a solution. Some unusually sensible people — the kind who read documentation and test their code and are consequently ignored at most meetings — discovered that if you stopped asking the Engine what it *knew* and started giving it things to *read*, the results improved dramatically.
26	
27	Give it a database. Give it documentation. Give it verified sources. Let it reason over real information instead of generating probable fictions from its vast internal library of Everything Humans Have Ever Written, Including The Wrong Parts.
28	
29	The Engine, thus grounded, became genuinely useful. Not because it became smarter. Not because it became trustworthy. But because it was now, for the first time, working with actual facts rather than vibes.****
30	
31	****\*Technical term.*
32	
33	The lesson, as with most important lessons, was short enough to fit on a Post-it note and complicated enough that most people would ignore it:
34	
35	**Don't ask the machine what's true. Give it the truth and ask it to think.**
36	
37	The machine, sensibly, had no opinion on any of this. It was, after all, just completing sequences.
38

file.content:
# On the Subject of Artificial Thinking Engines, and Why They Don't

It is a well-established fact* that humans will trust anything that sounds confident enough.

*\*It is not, in fact, an established fact. But it sounded confident, which rather proves the point.*

The Artificial Thinking Engine — or, as the people who built it preferred to call it, the Large Language Model, because naming things accurately would be bad for business — was the most impressive machine ever constructed for the purpose of sounding like it knew things. It did not, in any meaningful sense, know things. What it did was considerably more clever and considerably more dangerous: it *completed sentences*.

You gave it the beginning of a sentence. It gave you the most statistically probable ending. That was the entire trick. The entire, multi-billion-dollar, reshaping-the-global-economy trick. A completion engine with very good taste in next words.

"But it writes code!" said the engineers, who were very excited and had not yet been to production.

"But it explains complex topics!" said the managers, who were even more excited and had never been to production.

"But it sounds exactly like an expert!" said everyone, which was true in the same way that a parrot wearing a stethoscope sounds exactly like a doctor.**

**\*The parrot, at least, is unlikely to prescribe the wrong medication with perfect confidence. The Thinking Engine had no such restraint.*

The problem — and there was, inevitably, a problem, because there is always a problem when humans encounter something that tells them what they want to hear — was that the Engine was *equally confident when it was wrong*. It had to be. Confidence was not a feature it could adjust based on accuracy, because it had no access to accuracy. It had access to probability, which is related to accuracy in the same way that a map is related to the territory: usefully, but not reliably, and certainly not in the swamp bits.

The humans called it "hallucination" when the Engine was wrong, because that sounded like a temporary medical condition rather than a permanent architectural feature.***

***\*The word "hallucination" was chosen, one suspects, by the same type of person who calls a company-wide firing a "workforce optimization event." The Engine did not hallucinate. The Engine did exactly what it was designed to do. It completed sequences. Sometimes the sequences were true. Sometimes they weren't. The Engine could not tell the difference because the Engine did not have a concept of "difference." Or "true." Or "tell."*

There was, however, a solution. Some unusually sensible people — the kind who read documentation and test their code and are consequently ignored at most meetings — discovered that if you stopped asking the Engine what it *knew* and started giving it things to *read*, the results improved dramatically.

Give it a database. Give it documentation. Give it verified sources. Let it reason over real information instead of generating probable fictions from its vast internal library of Everything Humans Have Ever Written, Including The Wrong Parts.

The Engine, thus grounded, became genuinely useful. Not because it became smarter. Not because it became trustworthy. But because it was now, for the first time, working with actual facts rather than vibes.****

****\*Technical term.*

The lesson, as with most important lessons, was short enough to fit on a Post-it note and complicated enough that most people would ignore it:

**Don't ask the machine what's true. Give it the truth and ask it to think.**

The machine, sensibly, had no opinion on any of this. It was, after all, just completing sequences.

# What People Get Wrong About LLMs

March 2026

There's a pattern I keep seeing with people who use AI tools. Someone pastes an error message into ChatGPT, gets a plausible-sounding answer, and ships the fix. Someone else asks it to explain unfamiliar code, reads the response, and moves on. Then later they complain that it "made something up" or "did something I didn't ask for," as though they'd hired an employee who turned out to be incompetent.

I don't blame them. The interfaces are designed to feel like talking to a knowledgeable person. The responses are fluent, well-structured, and confident. Of course you'd mistake that for understanding. That's what fluency has meant for the entire history of human communication.

But it's a mistake, and a costly one, so it's worth understanding what's actually going on.

Large language models are completion engines. You give them a sequence of tokens and they predict what comes next, based on probability distributions learned during training. The key insight — the one most people miss — is that the training process optimizes for predicting likely next tokens, not for being correct. There's no retrieval mechanism. No fact-checking step. No internal distinction between "true" and "plausible." Every output is a statistical continuation. That's it.

What makes this interesting is how often those continuations happen to be right. They follow grammar. They produce working code. They solve math problems. They carry on natural conversations. This emergent coherence is genuinely surprising, and it's where the confusion starts. We see fluent, accurate output and pattern-match it to "this thing knows what it's talking about." But when the model produces something correct, it's because the training data happened to create probability distributions that align with reality. The model isn't looking anything up. It's just doing completion that, from a certain angle, looks like knowledge.

This brings us to what people call "hallucination," and I think the framing is almost exactly backwards.

We talk about hallucination as though it's the exception — as though the system normally gets things right and occasionally slips up. But that misrepresents the mechanism entirely. Completion is the default behavior. The model is always generating statistically plausible continuations. Sometimes those continuations happen to be true. Sometimes they don't. The model has no way to tell the difference.

The real surprise isn't that LLMs hallucinate. The real surprise is that they ever produce anything accurate at all.

What we call hallucination is just the base behavior becoming visible. The model generates something plausible but wrong, and we notice. But it was doing the same thing when it was right — generating plausible completions. We just didn't notice then because plausible and correct happened to overlap.

So when you paste a question into a chat interface and get a confident answer, here's what actually happened: a probability engine produced the most statistically likely continuation of your prompt. Nobody looked anything up. Nobody verified anything. Sometimes the continuation is true. Sometimes it isn't. And unless you check, you can't tell which.

This is why grounding matters. Instead of treating an LLM as a source of knowledge, you treat it as a reasoning engine and point it at actual sources — documentation, databases, APIs, search results. You let it retrieve and reason over real information rather than generating answers from probability distributions.

That's a genuinely different thing. The model goes from being an oracle you hope is right to a tool you can verify. And that shift — from trusting the output to grounding the output — changes everything about how you should build with AI.

Most of the mistakes I see people make with LLMs come from not understanding this distinction. They're treating a completion engine like a knowledge base. And the better the completions get, the harder that mistake is to catch.

content.content:
1	# What We Talk About When We Talk About Hallucination (Or: The Necessarily Recursive Problem of Trusting a System That Cannot Distinguish Between What It Knows and What It Invents, Which Turns Out to Be the Same Thing)
2	
3	There's a thing that happens — and if you've spent any time at all working with large language models you've almost certainly either witnessed it or done it yourself, and probably both — where a person, let's call them a software engineer because that's usually what they are, pastes some error message or chunk of unfamiliar code into a chat interface[^1] and receives back a response that is, by every surface-level metric we instinctively use to evaluate competence (fluency, structure, confidence, appropriate use of technical terminology), indistinguishable from expert advice. And so they use it. They ship it. They move on with their day.
4	
5	[^1]: Almost always ChatGPT, though increasingly Claude or Gemini or one of the other interfaces that have, through what is either very good design or very good marketing or both, managed to make "pasting text into a box and trusting what comes back" feel like a professional workflow rather than the epistemological coin-flip it actually is.
6	
7	And then later — sometimes hours, sometimes days, sometimes in a production incident that generates its own Slack channel — it turns out the response was wrong. Not approximately wrong. Not "wrong in a way that a reasonable person might have been wrong." Wrong in the specific way that a system which has no concept of correctness can be wrong, which is to say: wrong with complete confidence, wrong with perfect grammar, wrong in a way that *looked* exactly like being right.
8	
9	At which point the engineer says something like "it hallucinated," and everyone nods, and the conversation moves on to mitigation.
10	
11	But here's what's interesting[^2], and I think genuinely underappreciated, about that framing:
12	
13	[^2]: And by "interesting" I mean something closer to "alarming" or possibly "the central epistemological problem of our decade," though those phrases tend to make people stop reading, which is the opposite of what I need them to do right now.
14	
15	The word "hallucination" implies a system that normally perceives reality and occasionally, pathologically, departs from it. The way a person with a fever might see things that aren't there. The implication is: baseline = accurate, exception = fabrication.
16	
17	This is, almost exactly, backwards.
18	
19	A large language model is a completion engine. That's the whole thing[^3]. You provide a sequence of tokens and it predicts, based on probability distributions learned during training, what the next token should be. Then the next. Then the next. The training process — which involves processing an almost inconceivably large corpus of human-generated text — optimizes for one thing and one thing only: predicting the most likely next token. Not the most *correct* next token. Not the most *true* next token. The most *likely* one.
20	
21	[^3]: I realize this is a simplification and that people who work on transformer architectures will object to various aspects of this characterization. They're not wrong to object. But the simplification captures something essential about the failure mode, which is what matters here. The nuances of attention mechanisms and positional encoding are fascinating but do not change the fundamental point, which is that the system optimizes for statistical plausibility, not factual accuracy.
22	
23	There is no retrieval mechanism. There is no fact-checking step. There is no subroutine that runs after completion and before output that asks "but is this true?" The architecture does not distinguish between "true" and "plausible." It cannot. Truth is not a feature of the probability distribution. It's orthogonal to it.
24	
25	So what we call "hallucination" — the model generating something that sounds right but isn't — is not a departure from normal operation. It *is* normal operation. It's the base behavior. Every output, correct or incorrect, is produced by the same mechanism: statistical completion.
26	
27	The miracle[^4] — and I use that word with some precision — is that this process produces anything accurate at all.
28	
29	[^4]: Genuinely. If you had described the architecture of a transformer model to a computer scientist in 2005 and told them what it would be capable of in twenty years, they would have assumed you were either lying or confused. The emergent properties of these systems are, in a very real sense, not fully explained by current theory. Which should maybe make us more cautious about trusting them, not less.
30	
31	When the model produces a correct answer, that's because the training data happened to create probability distributions that align with reality. When it produces an incorrect answer, the same process generated the same *type* of output — statistically plausible completion — and it just happened not to align with reality that time. The mechanism is identical. Only the outcome varies.
32	
33	This has a practical implication that I think most people and most teams building with AI have not fully internalized:
34	
35	**You cannot improve the trustworthiness of an LLM by improving the LLM.** You can make the completions better — more often aligned with reality — but you cannot make the system *know when it's wrong*, because the distinction between right and wrong is not encoded in the architecture. Rightness is not a signal the model has access to.
36	
37	What you *can* do is ground it. Point the model at verified sources — documentation, databases, APIs, search results — and let it reason over retrieved information rather than generating answers from its probability distributions. This is a fundamentally different mode of operation. The model stops being a source of knowledge and becomes a tool for processing knowledge that comes from elsewhere.
38	
39	That shift — from oracle to tool — is, I think, the single most important architectural decision in any AI system that needs to be right[^5].
40	
41	[^5]: Which, to be clear, is all of them. There is no production use case where "probably right" is acceptable and "verifiably right" is not preferred. Even in creative applications — writing, brainstorming, ideation — grounding the model in real constraints and real information produces better creative output. The romantic idea that AI creativity benefits from unconstrained hallucination is, itself, a hallucination.
42	
43	Most of the mistakes I see teams make with LLMs — the production incidents, the confident-but-wrong outputs, the slowly accumulating technical debt of systems built on unverified completions — come from not understanding this distinction. They're treating a completion engine like a knowledge base. They're asking "what's the answer?" when they should be asking "given this information, what's the answer?"
44	
45	The difference is one word. The implications are architectural.
46
