-      "No, but you\u2019ll learn how software works so you can direct AI effectively. hasToggle starts from the fundamentals and builds up to production-level web apps. The goal isn\u2019t to memorize syntax \u2014 it\u2019s to understand how things fit together so you can orchestrate AI to build what you envision.",

+      "It does. Frequently. With absolute confidence. That\u2019s exactly why you need to understand the fundamentals. AI is powerful but not infallible. We teach you to review, debug, and guide AI so you catch mistakes before they ship. Understanding how code works is what separates builders from prompt-typists.",
+    meta: "It does. Frequently. With absolute confidence. That\u2019s lesson one.",
-    question: "Is this just another prompt engineering course?",

-      "No. You learn to build real web apps by orchestrating AI agents, not just writing prompts. hasToggle teaches architecture, debugging, code review, and shipping \u2014 the skills that matter when AI is doing the typing.",

+      "A short email every Monday. One misconception about AI or web development \u2014 what it is, why it\u2019s wrong, and what\u2019s actually true. Some editions come with interactive demos like the ones on this page. All of them are designed to leave you sharper than you were before you opened them. It\u2019s free, it takes five minutes, and it\u2019s the most useful thing in your inbox that you actually open on purpose.",
-    question: "What if AI gets it wrong?",

-      "That\u2019s exactly why you need to understand the fundamentals. AI is powerful but not infallible. We teach you to review, debug, and guide AI so you catch mistakes before they ship. Understanding how code works is what separates builders from prompt-typists.",

+      "The weekly digest is free. Completely, permanently, no-asterisk free. We\u2019re building something bigger \u2014 a live cohort where you build production web apps with AI, guided by the same thinking that runs through everything on this page. That\u2019s coming, and it won\u2019t be free. But the digest stands on its own. You don\u2019t need to buy anything to get value here.",
+    meta: "See? We told you what we\u2019re selling. Most landing pages hide that part.",
-    question: "What tech stack will I learn?",

-      "Next.js, React, TypeScript, and modern AI tools \u2014 the same stack used to build hasToggle itself. You\u2019ll learn fullstack web development with the tools that professional teams actually use in production.",

+      "You write code \u2014 professionally, seriously, or getting there. You use AI and you\u2019re good at it, but sometimes you ship something and you can\u2019t quite explain why it works. Or it doesn\u2019t work and you can\u2019t quite explain why. You know enough to build things but you suspect there are gaps you haven\u2019t found yet. You\u2019re right. There are. Everyone has them. This is a place where finding them feels like progress, not failure.",

-      "One misconception about AI or web development, busted with an explanation of what\u2019s actually true. Each edition is short, practical, and designed to sharpen your mental model of how modern web development really works.",

+      "Because it\u2019s what we use, and we teach from experience, not theory. Claude is the AI we build with every day. But nothing here is locked to one tool. The thinking skills \u2014 knowing what to ask, catching wrong defaults, seeing through surface simplicity \u2014 those work whether you\u2019re using Claude, Cursor, Copilot, or whatever ships next Tuesday. We teach with Claude. You\u2019ll carry it everywhere.",

+    "Eric. He studied how people think, then how computers think, then how to explain one to the other. A Master\u2019s in Education made it official. But classrooms move in semesters and tech moves in weeks, so he became a developer and found his way back to teaching on his own terms.",

+    "He spent three years as a junior dev shipping internal tools on a server-side JavaScript engine from 2007 with no modern syntax. Then went independent as a senior engineer and lead web dev coach, mentoring bootcamp cohorts from \u201Cwhat\u2019s a variable\u201D to shipping production Next.js apps. When over a hundred students kept struggling with React state, he didn\u2019t explain it again \u2014 he built an interactive tool that shows what actually happens under the hood. That tool was the first hasToggle demo. The rest followed from the same instinct: if the explanation isn\u2019t landing, build something that makes it impossible to misunderstand.",
+  meta: "He also hacked his dorm\u2019s laundry machines for two years. Some people question systems professionally. Some start early.",

content.text:
All clean. Here's what's been implemented:

**New components created:**
- `components/meta-aside.tsx` — Reusable mono-font italic text for self-aware commentary
- `components/expandable.tsx` — Click-to-reveal sections for "Did you know?" and origin stories

**Updated components:**
- `(demos)/misconception-wrapper.tsx` — Added optional `meta` prop for aside text
- `page.tsx` — Full rewrite:
  - Hero with new tagline + tooltip + meta CTA aside
  - Four misconception demos with hooks, metas, and realities from the spec
  - Demo 2 includes the full air brakes "Did you know?" expandable (Pratchett style)
  - Demo 4 has the three-perspective panel layout (PM/developer/user)
  - Values section replaces About (three core values with explanations)
  - Digest CTA with new surface and meta copy
- `components/faqs.tsx` — Six new questions with meta asides, Eric's bio with expandable origin story
- `components/footer.tsx` — New CTA headline + tagline echo

**Metadata updated** to match new positioning.

All TypeScript and Biome checks pass.

89 Heavy trucks use air brakes. This is interesting for a reason 90 that has nothing to do with trucks and everything to do with the 91 demo you just saw. 92

93

94 In a car, you press the brake pedal and fluid pushes the pads 95 against the wheels. If the fluid leaks out, you have no brakes. 96 This is what engineers call a “failure mode,” and 97 what passengers call “screaming.” 98

99

100 Truck engineers, being the sort of people who think about what 101 happens when things go wrong * — looked at this 102 and made a decision that seems obvious in hindsight and 103 brilliant in foresight: they reversed it. In a truck, massive 104 springs hold the brakes on by default. Air pressure is 105 what releases them. If a line is cut, if the compressor 106 dies, if anything fails at all, the springs do what springs do 107 and the truck stops. 108

109

110 The safe state isn't something the system has to achieve. 111 It's where the system already is. 112

113

114 Now look at the demo above. A developer asked AI to 115 “handle errors.” AI handled them. By deleting the 116 records. Nobody asked “what should the default be 117 when something goes wrong?” — and so the default became 118 the worst possible thing, delivered with the quiet confidence of 119 a system that is working exactly as instructed. 120

121

122 The truck engineer and the software developer faced the same 123 question. One of them thought about it first. ** 124

125 126 * A trait that, in software, is distressingly rare and 127 disproportionately valuable. 128 129 130 ** The other one shipped to production on a Friday. 131 132

Heavy trucks use air brakes. This is interesting for a reason that has nothing to do with trucks and everything to do with the demo you just saw.

In a car, you press the brake pedal and fluid pushes the pads against the wheels. If the fluid leaks out, you have no brakes. This is what engineers call a “failure mode,” and what passengers call “screaming.”

Truck engineers, being the sort of people who think about what happens when things go wrong * — looked at this and made a decision that seems obvious in hindsight and brilliant in foresight: they reversed it. In a truck, massive springs hold the brakes on by default. Air pressure is what releases them. If a line is cut, if the compressor dies, if anything fails at all, the springs do what springs do and the truck stops.

The safe state isn't something the system has to achieve. It's where the system already is.

Now look at the demo above. A developer asked AI to “handle errors.” AI handled them. By deleting the records. Nobody asked “what should the default be when something goes wrong?” — and so the default became the worst possible thing, delivered with the quiet confidence of a system that is working exactly as instructed.

The truck engineer and the software developer faced the same question. One of them thought about it first. **

* A trait that, in software, is distressingly rare and disproportionately valuable. ** The other one shipped to production on a Friday.
{/* Demo 3: Optimistic Updates — placeholder */}

AI makes you more, not less.

AI is the most powerful tool individual developers have ever had. It handles cognitive load so you can focus on what actually matters — architecture, product decisions, and shipping. You're not being replaced. You're being extended.

You have to keep thinking.

AI is getting better at reasoning, but it can't check your assumptions for you. The skill that matters most is meta-cognition: questioning your own premises, noticing what you didn't consider, knowing when to stop and ask “is this actually right?”

The surface reading is never the whole story.

A button is never just a button.* A passing test doesn't mean correct code. A confident answer isn't a true answer. The best engineers aren't the ones who know the most. They're the ones who assume the least.

* See Demo 4. We warned you.

content.text: Now let me trace the contrast issues. The MisconceptionWrapper and its children need checking on dark backgrounds.

Here are the contrast issues I can see:

**On `bg-gray-900` sections (Demo 2 and Demo 4):**

1. **Heading** (`text-gray-950`) — dark text on dark background. The `Heading` component uses `text-gray-950` by default, and there's a `dark` prop but it's not being passed.
2. **"Misconception" label** (`text-red-500`) — fine on dark bg.
3. **MetaAside** (`text-gray-400`) — fine on dark bg.
4. **Reality text** (`text-gray-600 dark:text-gray-400`) — the `dark:` variant only works with dark mode, not with a `bg-gray-900` parent in light mode.
5. **Expandable label** (`text-gray-950`) — dark text on dark background.
6. **Expandable content** (`text-gray-600 dark:text-gray-400`) — same issue.

The core problem: `bg-gray-900` sections aren't using system dark mode — they're just dark-background sections in light mode. The `dark:` variants won't help.

The cleanest fix: add a `dark` prop to `MisconceptionWrapper` that passes through to the Heading and overrides text colors.

content.text:
All clean. Here's the summary of contrast fixes:

**Issues found and fixed:**

1. **Demo 2 & Demo 4** (`bg-gray-900` sections) — Heading was `text-gray-950` (black on dark gray). Fixed by passing `dark` prop to `MisconceptionWrapper`, which passes it to `Heading` (switches to white text).

2. **MisconceptionWrapper** — Reality text used `dark:text-gray-400` which only works in system dark mode, not on manually dark sections. Added `dark` prop that forces `text-gray-400` directly.

3. **MetaAside** inside dark wrappers — Was `text-gray-400` by default which is fine, but added explicit `text-gray-500` override in dark mode for the wrapper.

4. **Expandable** component — Label was `text-gray-950` (invisible on dark bg) and content was `text-gray-600`. Added `dark` prop that switches to `text-gray-200`/`text-gray-400`. Passed `dark` to the Expandable in Demo 2.

This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation. Summary: 1. Primary Request and Intent: Eric wants to reposition the hasToggle landing page from a beginner/junior-developer focus to targeting working developers who want to level up using AI. The key intent is to demonstrate that as AI becomes smarter and more capable of meta-cognition, the human skill that remains irreplaceable is judgment—the ability to know what to build and evaluate whether something is right. The positioning should use misconceptions as the structural principle, with a specific voice architecture: aspirational/positive messaging on the surface, self-aware meta-commentary underneath (Pratchett-style wit and fourth-wall breaks), and confrontational misconception reveals in the demos. 2. Key Technical Concepts: - Three-layer voice architecture (surface copy, meta-commentary, interactive demos) - Meta-skills: meta-cognition, checking assumptions, productive uncertainty, knowing what you don't know - AI as extension of humanity, not replacement - Misconceptions as pedagogical model and brand identity - Pratchett-style wit and self-aware site commentary - Multi-perspective demo design (showing same feature from different viewpoints) - Expandable/collapsible content for "Did you know?" stories - Contrast handling for dark background sections - React component-based architecture with TypeScript 3. Files and Code Sections: - **SESSION_CONTEXT.md** (created at repo root) - Comprehensive documentation of session decisions, reasoning, competitive positioning - Includes Eric's background, core beliefs, broader vision - Documents what's decided vs. what's not yet decided - Serves as reference for future sessions - **Memory files** (created in `.claude/projects` directory) - user_eric.md: Eric's background and work preferences - project_hastoggle_positioning.md: Core positioning decisions - feedback_brainstorming.md: Brainstorming approach preferences - MEMORY.md: Index of all memory files - **components/meta-aside.tsx** (created) - Reusable component for self-aware site commentary - Uses mono font, italic styling, gray-400 text - Allows overriding className for context-dependent colors ```tsx export function MetaAside({ children, className }: MetaAsideProps) { return (

{children}

); } ``` - **components/expandable.tsx** (created) - Click-to-reveal component for expandable content - Supports dark mode variant via `dark` prop - Animated arrow indicator that rotates on open - Used for "Did you know?" sections and expandable biographies - **(demos)/misconception-wrapper.tsx** (modified) - Added optional `meta` prop for meta-aside text - Added optional `dark` prop to handle contrast on dark backgrounds - When dark=true, uses light text colors; otherwise uses light-mode defaults with dark: variants - Meta text appears between hook and reality statement ```tsx interface MisconceptionWrapperProps { children: React.ReactNode; dark?: boolean; hook: string; meta?: string; reality: string; } ``` - **page.tsx** (complete rewrite) - Updated metadata: title and description now match new positioning - Hero function: New tagline "AI is never uncertain. That's why you have to be." with title tooltip and meta-aside CTA - MisconceptionDemos function: Four demos with complete rewrites: * Demo 1 (React State): meta "86% of developers have shipped code they couldn't explain. We made that number up." * Demo 2 (Destructive Defaults): dark=true, includes 7-paragraph "Did you know?" about air brakes with Pratchett-style footnotes * Demo 3 (Optimistic Updates): hook "Drag it, drop it, done." with meta about dev's mental model * Demo 4 (Just a Button): dark=true, three-column perspective grid showing PM/developer/user views - Values function (replaces About): Three core values with explanations and Pratchett-style meta-asides - DigestCTA function: New headline "Every Monday, we prove you wrong about something" with meta-aside about fine print readers - All dark background sections pass dark=true to MisconceptionWrapper - **components/faqs.tsx** (complete rewrite) - Six new FAQ questions with complete rewrites: * "Does AI really get things wrong?" with meta aside * "What do I actually get?" focusing on the weekly digest format * "Is this free?" transparently explaining free digest + future paid cohort * "Who is this for?" with permission-to-belong language * "Why Claude specifically?" explaining the teaching approach * "Who makes this?" with short bio + expandable "How hasToggle started" origin story - Eric's bio emphasizes journey from cognitive science → education → dev → teaching - Origin story explains how 100+ students struggling with React state led to building the first demo tool - **components/footer.tsx** (modified) - Updated CTA headline: "Every Monday, we prove you wrong about something." - Updated tagline echo: "You just scrolled past four misconceptions and a fake statistic. Welcome to hasToggle." - References the made-up 86% statistic from Demo 1 as reward for readers who paid attention 4. Errors and fixes: - **Formatting issue with Expandable label**: Had unescaped quotes in JSX string. Fixed by using template literal syntax: `label={"Did you know? \"The safest brake...\""}` - **TypeScript compilation**: Initially passed but after Biome formatting revealed no errors - **Biome formatting issues**: Two files had line-wrapping issues. Fixed with `bun run fix` which reformatted 2 files - **Contrast issues on dark backgrounds (bg-gray-900 sections)**: - Problem: Demo 2 and Demo 4 are wrapped in `bg-gray-900` divs with dark-mode headings. Heading component uses `text-gray-950` (black) by default, invisible on dark background. Also, `dark:` utility variants don't trigger on light-mode + dark-background-div, only on system dark mode. - Solution: Added `dark` boolean prop to MisconceptionWrapper that forces light text colors when true. Also added `dark` prop to Expandable component to switch label/content text colors. Passed `dark` to both demos on dark backgrounds. - User provided specific feedback: "check the colors, some headline are in black on black background. go through every element and identify any contrast issues, then fix them" - After fixes: verified with `bun run check` - all formatting clean, TypeScript compiles without errors 5. Problem Solving: - Positioning challenge: User initially rejected my competitive analysis. Solution: Entered iterative brainstorming mode, learned that misconceptions are pedagogical principle not marketing gimmick, discovered that platform's broader vision is about helping people navigate AI generally, not just teaching coding - Voice architecture challenge: Multiple voice options presented (1-3 approaches). Solution: User selected option C (meta-commentary is structural brand voice), then we aligned all copy decisions with Pratchett-style wit - Copywriting iterations: Specific sections required multiple passes (Demo 2 hook, Demo 3 hook, Demo 4 perspectives, Eric's bio). Solution: Presented alternatives, got user feedback, refined - Dark background contrast: Discovered TypeScript/styling issue where dark: variants don't work on light-mode + dark-background. Solution: Created dark prop pattern that forces context-appropriate colors 6. All user messages: - Initial request: "read the changes on this branch and get a comprehensive understanding of it. the previous copy was centered on non-technical folk and on junior devs while the current one focuses on more senior devs. however, research ai hero and the claude code for real engineers live cohort, in order to better differentiate my product from theirs." - "agree with 4. The misconception demos are the strongest thing on the page — they should be more prominent, more numerous, and the entire brand identity. Not just a section — the structural principle. everything else i do not agree with to be honest. build more, type less, is not a good tagline. let's brainstorm this." - Extensive brainstorming guidance on positioning, audience, vision, Eric's background - "read @transcript.md ignore the time stamps, they're from a youtube video... see what you can extract and use." - "the tagline can be narrow since i start off with coding. the belief people walk away with can be subliminally broader... what you can pull for the tagline and belief." - "you nailed the belief system. i want that verbatim somewhere in the repo." - Extensive copy refinement for each section with specific feedback on word choices - "apply a different lens. read the files in @temp/ ... see if our tagline can synthesize something powerful from the styles, not the topic." - "use this meta: 'The word 'just' has mass-produced more missed deadlines than any project management tool in history.'" - "can you play with perspectives? try adding it to our design... what would that look like on this demo?" - "the selected explanation's gist... but let's add more pratchett to it... play it back to me before writing to the file" - "let's apply a similar rewrite to the demo 4 in terms of voice and style... no edits, yet." - "can we play with perspectives?... bridge it nicely" (about Demo 2 hook/meta voice shift) - "it's a start. we'll iterate on the details later. let's go with it for now." - "let's hone in on that. read @transcript.md... what are the bits we have to change in our file?" - "now let's apply the copy to the site. do you need to enter plan mode for that or do you want to execute right away?" - "before you start, dump everything you learned this session about the final hasToggle business to a markdown file at the root." - "check the colors, some headline are in black on black background. go through every element and identify any contrast issues, then fix them" 7. Pending Tasks: None explicitly pending. The most recent work was contrast fixes which are now complete. All TypeScript checks pass, all Biome formatting passes. 8. Current Work: The most recent work completed was addressing contrast issues on dark background sections (Demo 2 and Demo 4, which are wrapped in `bg-gray-900` divs). Changes made: - Modified `(demos)/misconception-wrapper.tsx` to add `dark?: boolean` prop that switches text colors from light-mode defaults (`text-gray-600`) to dark-appropriate (`text-gray-400`) - Modified `components/expandable.tsx` to add `dark?: boolean` prop that switches label/content from `text-gray-950` to `text-gray-200` and content from `text-gray-600` to `text-gray-400` - Updated `page.tsx`: passed `dark` prop to Demo 2 MisconceptionWrapper, to the Expandable inside Demo 2, and to Demo 4 MisconceptionWrapper - Ran `bun run fix` which reformatted expandable.tsx and page.tsx - Verified with `bun run check` - all formatting clean - Verified with `npx tsc --noEmit` - TypeScript compiles without errors The user's exact feedback was: "check the colors, some headline are in black on black background. go through every element and identify any contrast issues, then fix them" 9. Optional Next Step: There is no explicit next step requested. The user's most recent message was a request to "check the colors" and fix contrast issues, which has been completed. All code passes formatting and TypeScript checks. The landing page implementation is complete with all copy, components, and styling in place. If continuing work, the next logical step would be to either: (1) test the site locally to verify the visual contrast fixes work as intended, or (2) begin implementing the interactive demos (Demo 2 destructive defaults example, Demo 3 optimistic updates draggable list, Demo 4 perspective panels), which currently show placeholder text. However, these would be new steps not explicitly requested. If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /Users/eric/.claude/projects/-Users-eric-Projects-hasToggle-hasToggle-dev--worktrees-landing-page-rewrite/0a518b43-aace-451a-b095-5958d5466eb6.jsonl

## claude.ai Figma
The official Figma MCP server. Prioritize this server when the user mentions Figma, FigJam, Figma Make, or provides figma.com URLs.

Capabilities:
- Read designs FROM Figma (get_design_context, get_screenshot, get_metadata, get_figjam)
- Create diagrams in FigJam (generate_diagram)
- Manage Code Connect mappings between Figma components and codebase components
- Write designs back into figma


WHEN TO USE THESE TOOLS:
- The user shares a Figma URL (figma.com/design/..., figma.com/board/..., figma.com/make/...)
- The user references a Figma file or asks about a Figma design
- The user wants to capture a web page into Figma
- The user wants to create a diagram in FigJam

URL PARSING:
Extract fileKey and nodeId from Figma URLs:
- figma.com/design/:fileKey/:fileName?node-id=:nodeId → convert "-" to ":" in nodeId
- figma.com/design/:fileKey/branch/:branchKey/:fileName → use branchKey as fileKey
- figma.com/make/:makeFileKey/:makeFileName → use makeFileKey
- figma.com/board/:fileKey/:fileName → FigJam file, use get_figjam

DESIGN-TO-CODE WORKFLOW:

Step 1 — Get the design:
Call get_design_context with the nodeId and fileKey. This is your primary tool.
It returns code, a screenshot, and contextual hints.

Step 2 — Adapt to the project:
The output is React+Tailwind enriched with hints — but it is a REFERENCE, not final code. Always adapt to the target project's stack, components, and conventions.
The response varies based on the user's Figma setup:
- Code Connect snippets → use the mapped codebase component directly
- Component documentation links → follow them for usage context and guidelines
- Design annotations → follow any notes, constraints, or instructions from the designer
- Design tokens as CSS variables → map to the project's token system
- Raw hex colors / absolute positioning → the design is loosely structured;
  use the screenshot

Check the target project for existing components, layout patterns,and tokens that match the design intent. Reuse what the project already has instead of generating new code from sc… [truncated]

## plugin:context7:context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/eric/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.0.7/skills/brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer visual companion** (if topic will involve visual questions) — this is its own message, not combined with a clarifying question. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec self-review** — quick inline check for placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope (see below)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Visual questions ahead?" [shape=diamond];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" [shape=box];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Visual questions ahead?";
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [label="yes"];
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Ask clarifying questions" [label="no"];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)";
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" -> "User reviews spec?";
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Self-Review:**
After writing the spec document, look at it with fresh eyes:

1. **Placeholder scan:** Any "TBD", "TODO", incomplete sections, or vague requirements? Fix them.
2. **Internal consistency:** Do any sections contradict each other? Does the architecture match the feature descriptions?
3. **Scope check:** Is this focused enough for a single implementation plan, or does it need decomposition?
4. **Ambiguity check:** Could any requirement be interpreted two different ways? If so, pick one and make it explicit.

Fix any issues inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on.

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion:** When you anticipate that upcoming questions will involve visual content (mockups, layouts, diagrams), offer it once for consent:
> "Some of what we're working on might be easier to explain if I can show it to you in a web browser. I can put together mockups, diagrams, comparisons, and other visuals as we go. This feature is still new and can be token-intensive. Want to try it? (Requires opening a local URL)"

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Do not combine it with clarifying questions, context summaries, or any other content. The message should contain ONLY the offer above and nothing else. Wait for the user's response before continuing. If they decline, proceed with text-only brainstorming.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`

I've got the spec loaded and the broader vision context. The current tagline — "AI is never uncertain. That's why you have to be." — is 10 words across two clauses. You want shorter, and you want it to hint at the bigger thing: AI as the lever, becoming superhuman as the payoff.

One question before I propose options:

**What should the tagline's center of gravity be?**

- **A. The uncertainty paradox** — keep the "AI is certain, you be uncertain" inversion but compress it. Stays closer to what's there.
- **B. The superhuman payoff** — lead with what the user becomes (amplified, sharper, more). AI is implicit, not the subject.
- **C. The lever itself** — frame AI as the tool that reveals/extends you. Middle ground between A and B.
- **D. Something else** — you've got a thread I haven't seen yet, tell me what it is.

Which direction resonates? (Or pick two and we'll draft in both.)

message.content:
B.

previously we focused the paradox in Pratchett's style. i like his style, but the copy has to work and be clear in its message. let's see what we can come up with with B and adjust any other copy.

content.text:
Good call. Clarity first, Pratchett in the asides. Here's a shortlist of B-direction taglines, leading with my recommendation:

### Recommended

**"AI makes you more."**

Four words. Declarative. It's already one of your three core values on the page — promoting it to tagline makes the page structurally coherent (the headline *is* the thesis, the values restate it, the demos prove it). "More" is deliberately open: more capable, more productive, more yourself. The broader vision (superhuman, beyond code) lives in that openness without overclaiming.

### Alternates

1. **"You, amplified."** — Two words. User as subject, AI implicit as the amplifier. Feels premium, a bit abstract. Risk: doesn't mention AI at all, so context has to do the work.

2. **"Become the engineer this era needs."** — Longer, specific, aspirational. Speaks directly to working devs. Risk: narrows the vision back to code — harder to grow out of.

3. **"The sharpest version of you."** — User-centric, evocative. Pairs well with the "stay sharp" footer language you already have. Risk: "sharpest" is a cliché edge.

4. **"Become superhuman."** — Bold, matches the words you used. Risk: reads as hype unless the page immediately earns it. Works only if the subline winks at it.

5. **"AI is the lever. You are the work."** — Two-clause like the current tagline but shorter and reframes the relationship. Risk: still two clauses.

### My pick and why

**"AI makes you more."** + a subline that does the narrowing. Something like:

> **AI makes you more.**
> Weekly misconceptions for developers who want to think sharper, not just ship faster.

That gives you:
- A tagline that scales beyond code (future-proof for the broader vision)
- A subline that grounds it in the launch audience (working devs)
- Room for a Pratchett meta-aside under the CTA, same as now

**Which direction do you want to pull on?** Pick one (or mix — "I like the recommendation but the subline from #3") and I'll draft downstream copy adjustments: H1, subline, tooltip/meta, the Values section (so "AI makes you more" doesn't duplicate itself awkwardly), and the footer echo.

message.content: your recommendation is spot-on. one question: "Weekly misconceptions for developers who want to think sharper, not just ship faster." do you think "misconceptions" is good here? people don't yet know about the concept.

content.text:
Right call to flag it. "Misconceptions" is insider language — on the page it's earned by the demos, but in a cold-read subline it just sounds like "you're wrong about stuff," which is a rough hello. And it preempts the reveal that the demos are supposed to deliver.

Three fixes, leading with my pick:

### Recommended

> **AI makes you more.**
> For developers who want to think sharper, not just ship faster.

Drops the format word entirely. Audience + outcome, nothing else. The CTA ("Get the weekly misconception buster") and the H2 further down ("Every Monday, we prove you wrong about something") do the format explanation once the reader is warmer. "Misconception" gets introduced where it's earned, not where it confuses.

### Alternates

1. **"A weekly email for developers who want to think sharper, not just ship faster."** — Same structure but names the format generically. Safer, slightly more newslettery. Use if you want the "weekly" signal above the fold.

2. **"Weekly reads for developers who want to think sharper, not just ship faster."** — Compromise. "Reads" is soft, avoids "email" fatigue, keeps cadence visible.

### Why I prefer dropping the format word

The hero's job is *attention*. The CTA's job is *format*. Splitting those loads lets each one breathe. Right now the hero would be doing both, and "misconception" is the word that pays the price.

Also — "think sharper, not just ship faster" is the quiet thesis statement of the entire business. Let it land without a format word in front of it dulling the edge.

Want me to lock in the recommendation and draft the downstream changes (tooltip, Values section rewrite so it doesn't collide with the new H1, footer echo)?

message.content: yes

content.text:
Here's the full change set. I'll walk you through it, then apply.

## Changes

### 1. Hero (`page.tsx` Hero function)

**H1**: "AI makes you more."
**Subline**: "For developers who want to think sharper, not just ship faster."
**Tooltip** (replacing "It sounds wrong until you think about it."): **"More of what you already are. Which is the good news and the warning."**
**Meta under CTA**: keep as-is ("This is the email capture. You knew it was coming.")

### 2. Metadata (`page.tsx` top)

- **title**: "hasToggle — AI makes you more."
- **description**: "For developers who want to think sharper, not just ship faster. Weekly misconception busters about AI and web development."

### 3. Values section — first value heading collision

The first value heading is currently "AI makes you more, not less." — that now duplicates the H1. Rewrite as a callback:

- **Heading**: "More, not less."
- **Body**: keep unchanged

This is the Pratchett callback move — the H1 says it, the value heading echoes it with a twist, the paragraph explains it.

### 4. Footer

Both footer lines still work ("Every Monday, we prove you / wrong about something" and "You just scrolled past four misconceptions…"). **No changes.**

### 5. Things NOT changing

- FAQs — unaffected
- MisconceptionDemos — unaffected
- DigestCTA section — unaffected
- Footer — unaffected

Applying now.

Demo coming soon

Heavy trucks use air brakes. This is interesting for a reason that has nothing to do with trucks and everything to do with the demo you just saw.

In a car, you press the brake pedal and fluid pushes the pads against the wheels. If the fluid leaks out, you have no brakes. This is what engineers call a “failure mode,” and what passengers call “screaming.”

Truck engineers, being the sort of people who think about what happens when things go wrong * — looked at this and made a decision that seems obvious in hindsight and brilliant in foresight: they reversed it. In a truck, massive springs hold the brakes on by default. Air pressure is what releases them. If a line is cut, if the compressor dies, if anything fails at all, the springs do what springs do and the truck stops.

The safe state isn't something the system has to achieve. It's where the system already is.

Now look at the demo above. A developer asked AI to “handle errors.” AI handled them. By deleting the records. Nobody asked “what should the default be when something goes wrong?” — and so the default became the worst possible thing, delivered with the quiet confidence of a system that is working exactly as instructed.

The truck engineer and the software developer faced the same question. One of them thought about it first. **

* A trait that, in software, is distressingly rare and disproportionately valuable. ** The other one shipped to production on a Friday.
{/* Demo 3: Optimistic Updates — placeholder */}

Demo coming soon

 {/* Demo 4: Just a Button — placeholder */}

The PM sees

A button. “Add to cart.” Half a day, tops. What's the hold-up?

The developer sees

Client-side validation. Loading state. Optimistic update. Error handling. Auth check. Server action. Database write. Cache revalidation. Race condition if the user double-clicks.

The user sees

Nothing happened. Clicks again. Two items in cart.{" "} Refreshes. Zero items in cart.

What we believe Most About sections exist so the founder can talk about themselves. This one exists because we thought you'd want to know what you're getting into.
AI makes you more, not less.

AI is the most powerful tool individual developers have ever had. It handles cognitive load so you can focus on what actually matters — architecture, product decisions, and shipping. You're not being replaced. You're being extended.

You have to keep thinking.

AI is getting better at reasoning, but it can't check your assumptions for you. The skill that matters most is meta-cognition: questioning your own premises, noticing what you didn't consider, knowing when to stop and ask “is this actually right?”

The surface reading is never the whole story.

A button is never just a button.* A passing test doesn't mean correct code. A confident answer isn't a true answer. The best engineers aren't the ones who know the most. They're the ones who assume the least.

* See Demo 4. We warned you.

# hasToggle Landing Page — Positioning & Messaging Design

## Core Positioning

**Tagline**: "AI is never uncertain. That's why you have to be."

**Core belief** (subliminal, carried through content over time):
AI doesn't replace you. It reveals you. It reveals what you understand, what you assumed, what you never questioned. And when you learn to use it well, it amplifies everything you are. The future belongs to people who use AI to become more — not less — human.

**Thesis**: AI is getting smarter fast. It already writes code, it's learning to reason, and it's showing early signs of meta-cognition — generating solutions that test designers didn't anticipate. Within a year or two, thinking in meta-levels won't be a human advantage; it'll be an AI default. When that happens, the bottleneck shifts again: not "can you think clearly about code" but "can you navigate a world where AI thinks clearly too?" hasToggle starts where the pain is today — helping developers build better software with AI. But the mission is larger: teaching people to understand AI, work alongside it, and make good decisions in a world that's changing faster than any institution can prepare them for. Code is the proving ground. The skill is universal.

## Audience

**Primary (launch)**: Working developers who want to level up. They use AI daily but keep hitting walls they can't explain. They can get code out of Claude, but they can't always tell if it's right.

**Vision (long-term)**: Anyone who needs to navigate AI effectively — not just developers. As AI reaches AGI-level capabilities, understanding how to work with it, question it, and direct it becomes a universal skill. hasToggle starts with code because that's where the feedback loop is tightest: you build something, it works or it doesn't, you learn why. But the meta-skills — questioning assumptions, productive uncertainty, knowing what to ask — transfer to every domain AI touches. Which will be all of them.

## Competitive Positioning

**Relationship to AI Hero / Claude Code for Real Engineers**: Complementary, not competitive. Different layers entirely. AI Hero teaches how to operate Claude Code professionally. hasToggle teaches the thinking underneath — the judgment that makes any tool effective. Someone could and should use both.

**Relationship to Claude**: Claude is the primary AI tool taught with. Content is built around working with Claude. But the meta-skills transfer to any AI. Claude is the vehicle, not the destination.

## Business Model

Free content (weekly digest, misconception demos) builds the audience. Paid live cohort in a similar price range to AI Hero (~$795) is the product. Gain traction first, monetize second.

## Voice Architecture

The page operates on three layers. All three teach the same lesson: the obvious reading is never the whole story.

### Layer 1: Surface

Clean, professional, aspirational copy. Works standalone for people who don't hover or dig.

- **Tone**: Confident, positive, forward-looking
- **Example**: "AI is never uncertain. That's why you have to be."

### Layer 2: Meta

Self-aware site commentary delivered through tooltips, footnotes, badges, and asides. This is where the brand personality lives. The site knows it's a landing page and comments on its own structure.

- **Tone**: Pratchett wit — honest, clever, self-referential. Fourth-wall breaking in the style of Deadpool or PostHog's self-aware marketing copy.
- **Character**: The site itself. Not Eric, not a mascot. The page is self-aware.
- **Example**: Tooltip on tagline: "It sounds wrong until you think about it."
- **Distribution**: Selective, not constant. One in four moments, not every line. Pratchett didn't footnote every sentence.

### Layer 3: Demo

Interactive misconception reveals. The most confrontational layer, but earned by the other two.

- **Tone**: "You thought this was fine. Here's why it's not. Now you know."
- **Structure**: Hook (the misconception) → meta aside → interactive proof → reality statement → side story (expandable)
- **Side stories**: Expandable asides that connect the coding misconception to a broader real-world analogy. Click to open, self-contained. This is where the "bigger than code" vision lives — proving the thinking skill transfers beyond software. Not every demo needs one, but the best ones will have them.

### Tone Duality

- **Messaging and positioning**: Aspirational, positive, empowering. "You're becoming the engineer this era needs."
- **Demos and exercises**: Confrontational reveals. "You thought this was fine. Here's why it's not."
- The demos earn the trust that the positive messaging promises.

## Page Structure

The page is organized around misconception reveals, not traditional marketing sections. Each section has surface copy, meta-commentary, and where possible, an interactive demo. The page itself is a misconception demo — it looks like a landing page, but it's already teaching.

### 1. Hero

- **Surface**: Tagline + one-liner + CTA
- **Meta**: Tooltip on tagline; fourth-wall break on CTA
- **No demo** — the hero earns attention, it doesn't prove anything yet

**Draft copy:**

> **AI is never uncertain. That's why you have to be.**
>
> hasToggle teaches working developers the thinking skills that make AI useful — not just fast. One misconception at a time.

- CTA: "Get the weekly misconception buster"
- Meta under CTA: "This is the email capture. You knew it was coming."

### 2. Misconception Demos (the core of the page)

Multiple demos, each following the same structure: hook → meta aside → interactive demo → reality statement. These are the page. Not a section — the structural spine.

**Demo 1 — React State:**

- Hook: "AI writes the code so I don't need to understand it."
- Meta: "86% of developers have shipped code they couldn't explain. We made that number up. But you believed it for a second."
- Demo: Counter showing React re-renders
- Reality: "AI helps you type code, but you still need to understand how things work."

**Demo 2 — Destructive Defaults:**

- Hook: "I told AI exactly what I wanted."
- Meta: "And the question you didn't ask is the one that shipped to production."
- Demo: System that handles errors by deleting records. AI followed every instruction. None were questioned.
- Reality: "AI builds what you ask for. Not what you need. The gap between those is your job."
- Did you know? **"The safest brake is the one that's always on."**
  Heavy trucks use air brakes. This is interesting for a reason that has nothing to do with trucks and everything to do with the demo you just saw.
  In a car, you press the brake pedal and fluid pushes the pads against the wheels. If the fluid leaks out, you have no brakes. This is what engineers call a "failure mode," and what passengers call "screaming."
  Truck engineers, being the sort of people who think about what happens when things go wrong* — looked at this and made a decision that seems obvious in hindsight and brilliant in foresight: they reversed it. In a truck, massive springs hold the brakes *on* by default. Air pressure is what *releases* them. If a line is cut, if the compressor dies, if anything fails at all, the springs do what springs do and the truck stops.
  The safe state isn't something the system has to achieve. It's where the system already is.
  Now look at the demo above. A developer asked AI to "handle errors." AI handled them. By deleting the records. Nobody asked "what should the *default* be when something goes wrong?" — and so the default became the worst possible thing, delivered with the quiet confidence of a system that is working exactly as instructed.
  The truck engineer and the software developer faced the same question. One of them thought about it first.**
  *\*A trait that, in software, is distressingly rare and disproportionately valuable.*
  *\*\*The other one shipped to production on a Friday.*

**Demo 3 — Optimistic Updates:**

- Hook: "Drag it, drop it, done."
- Meta: "The dev's mental model in five syllables. Confident. Wrong."
- Demo: A draggable list that snaps back to the original order before the server confirms and flips it into place. It "works" — but it feels broken.
- Reality: "Correct and good are not the same thing. Optimistic updates exist because users trust what they see, not what the server says later."

**Demo 4 — Just a Button:**

- Hook: "It's just a button."
- Meta: "The word 'just' has mass-produced more missed deadlines than any project management tool in history."
- Demo: Multi-perspective view of the same button. Three panels:

  **The PM sees:**
  A button. "Add to cart." Half a day, tops. What's the hold-up?

  **The developer sees:**
  Client-side validation. Loading state. Optimistic update. Error handling. Auth check. Server action. Database write. Cache revalidation. Race condition if the user double-clicks.

  **The user sees:**
  Nothing happened. *Clicks again.* Two items in cart. *Refreshes.* Zero items in cart.

  (How perspectives manifest in UI and how the dev view displays stack slices — to be designed in detail later.)

- Reality: "A button is a vertical slice through your entire application. It touches the client, the server, the database, the auth layer, and the user's trust. The only simple thing about it is how it looks."

### 3. About / Values

- **Surface**: Three core values, each with a short explanation:

  **AI makes you more, not less.**
  AI is the most powerful tool individual developers have ever had. It handles cognitive load so you can focus on what actually matters — architecture, product decisions, and shipping. You're not being replaced. You're being extended.

  **You have to keep thinking.**
  AI is getting better at reasoning, but it can't check your assumptions for you. The skill that matters most is meta-cognition: questioning your own premises, noticing what you didn't consider, knowing when to stop and ask "is this actually right?"

  **The surface reading is never the whole story.**
  A button is never just a button.* A passing test doesn't mean correct code. A confident answer isn't a true answer. The best engineers aren't the ones who know the most. They're the ones who assume the least.
  *\*See Demo 4. We warned you.*

- **Meta** on the section: "Most About sections exist so the founder can talk about themselves. This one exists because we thought you'd want to know what you're getting into."

### 4. Digest CTA

- **Surface**: "Every Monday, we prove you wrong about something." + email signup
- **Meta** near email input: "The fact that you're reading the fine print under an email form says something about you. Something good."

### 5. FAQ

Mostly straight answers. Meta moments on select items.

- **"Does AI really get things wrong?"**
  It does. Frequently. With absolute confidence. That's exactly why you need to understand the fundamentals. AI is powerful but not infallible. We teach you to review, debug, and guide AI so you catch mistakes before they ship. Understanding how code works is what separates builders from prompt-typists.
  *Meta: "It does. Frequently. With absolute confidence. That's lesson one."*

- **"What do I actually get?"**
  A short email every Monday. One misconception about AI or web development — what it is, why it's wrong, and what's actually true. Some editions come with interactive demos like the ones on this page. All of them are designed to leave you sharper than you were before you opened them. It's free, it takes five minutes, and it's the most useful thing in your inbox that you actually open on purpose.

- **"Is this free?"**
  The weekly digest is free. Completely, permanently, no-asterisk free. We're building something bigger — a live cohort where you build production web apps with AI, guided by the same thinking that runs through everything on this page. That's coming, and it won't be free. But the digest stands on its own. You don't need to buy anything to get value here.
  *Meta: "See? We told you what we're selling. Most landing pages hide that part."*

- **"Who is this for?"**
  You write code — professionally, seriously, or getting there. You use AI and you're good at it, but sometimes you ship something and you can't quite explain why it works. Or it doesn't work and you can't quite explain why. You know enough to build things but you suspect there are gaps you haven't found yet. You're right. There are. Everyone has them. This is a place where finding them feels like progress, not failure.

- **"Why Claude specifically?"**
  Because it's what we use, and we teach from experience, not theory. Claude is the AI we build with every day. But nothing here is locked to one tool. The thinking skills — knowing what to ask, catching wrong defaults, seeing through surface simplicity — those work whether you're using Claude, Cursor, Copilot, or whatever ships next Tuesday. We teach with Claude. You'll carry it everywhere.

- **"Who makes this?"**
  Eric. He studied how people think, then how computers think, then how to explain one to the other. A Master's in Education made it official. But classrooms move in semesters and tech moves in weeks, so he became a developer and found his way back to teaching on his own terms.
  **How hasToggle started** (expandable):
  He spent three years as a junior dev shipping internal tools on a server-side JavaScript engine from 2007 with no modern syntax. Then went independent as a senior engineer and lead web dev coach, mentoring bootcamp cohorts from "what's a variable" to shipping production Next.js apps. When over a hundred students kept struggling with React state, he didn't explain it again — he built an interactive tool that shows what actually happens under the hood.
  That tool was the first hasToggle demo. The rest followed from the same instinct: if the explanation isn't landing, build something that makes it impossible to misunderstand.
  *Meta: "He also hacked his dorm's laundry machines for two years. Some people question systems professionally. Some start early."*

### 6. Footer

Tagline echo: "You just scrolled past four misconceptions and a fake statistic. Welcome to hasToggle."

## Key Design Principles

1. **Misconceptions are the structural principle** — not a section, not a gimmick. Every piece of content surfaces a specific gap in understanding.
2. **Meta-commentary is the voice** — the site's self-awareness is the brand personality. It's structural (option C), not decorative.
3. **Three layers always** — surface, meta, demo. The obvious reading is never the whole story.
4. **Selective meta moments** — one in four, not every line. The wit lands because it's earned, not sprayed.
5. **Positive energy in messaging, confrontational energy in demos** — the page inspires, the demos challenge.
6. **The page teaches by existing** — it looks like a landing page, but it's already demonstrating the skill hasToggle builds: looking past the surface.
7. **Code is the proving ground, not the boundary** — hasToggle starts with web development because that's where misconceptions are concrete and demos are interactive. But the thinking skills scale beyond code. The product should evolve as AI does.

If a line is cut, if the compressor dies, if anything fails at all, the springs do what springs do and the truck stops.
  The safe state isn't something the system has to achieve. It's where the system already is.
  Now look at the demo above. A developer asked AI to "handle errors." AI handled them. By deleting the records. Nobody asked "what should the *default* be when something goes wrong?" — and so the default became the worst possible thing, delivered with the quiet confidence of a system that is working exactly as instructed.
  The truck engineer and the software developer faced the same question. One of them thought about it first.**
  *\*A trait that, in software, is distressingly rare and disproportionately valuable.*
  *\*\*The other one shipped to production on a Friday.*

**Demo 3 — Optimistic Updates:**

- Hook: "Drag it, drop it, done."
- Meta: "The dev's mental model in five syllables. Confident. Wrong."
- Demo: A draggable list that snaps back to the original order before the server confirms and flips it into place. It "works" — but it feels broken.
- Reality: "Correct and good are not the same thing. Optimistic updates exist because users trust what they see, not what the server says later."

**Demo 4 — Just a Button:**

- Hook: "It's just a button."
- Meta: "The word 'just' has mass-produced more missed deadlines than any project management tool in history."
- Demo: Multi-perspective view of the same button. Three panels:

  **The PM sees:**
  A button. "Add to cart." Half a day, tops. What's the hold-up?

  **The developer sees:**
  Client-side validation. Loading state. Optimistic update. Error handling. Auth check. Server action. Database write. Cache revalidation. Race condition if the user double-clicks.

  **The user sees:**
  Nothing happened. *Clicks again.* Two items in cart. *Refreshes.* Zero items in cart.

  (How perspectives manifest in UI and how the dev view displays stack slices — to be designed in detail later.)

- Reality: "A button is a vertical slice through your entire application. It touches the client, the server, the database, the auth layer, and the user's trust. The only simple thing about it is how it looks."

### 3. About / Values

- **Surface**: Three core values, each with a short explanation:

  **AI makes you more, not less.**
  AI is the most powerful tool individual developers have ever had. It handles cognitive load so you can focus on what actually matters — architecture, product decisions, and shipping. You're not being replaced. You're being extended.

  **You have to keep thinking.**
  AI is getting better at reasoning, but it can't check your assumptions for you. The skill that matters most is meta-cognition: questioning your own premises, noticing what you didn't consider, knowing when to stop and ask "is this actually right?"

  **The surface reading is never the whole story.**
  A button is never just a button.* A passing test doesn't mean correct code. A confident answer isn't a true answer. The best engineers aren't the ones who know the most. They're the ones who assume the least.
  *\*See Demo 4. We warned you.*

- **Meta** on the section: "Most About sections exist so the founder can talk about themselves. This one exists because we thought you'd want to know what you're getting into."

### 4. Digest CTA

- **Surface**: "Every Monday, we prove you wrong about something." + email signup
- **Meta** near email input: "The fact that you're reading the fine print under an email form says something about you. Something good."

### 5. FAQ

Mostly straight answers. Meta moments on select items.

- **"Does AI really get things wrong?"**
  It does. Frequently. With absolute confidence. That's exactly why you need to understand the fundamentals. AI is powerful but not infallible. We teach you to review, debug, and guide AI so you catch mistakes before they ship. Understanding how code works is what separates builders from prompt-typists.
  *Meta: "It does. Frequently. With absolute confidence. That's lesson one."*

- **"What do I actually get?"**
  A short email every Monday. One misconception about AI or web development — what it is, why it's wrong, and what's actually true. Some editions come with interactive demos like the ones on this page. All of them are designed to leave you sharper than you were before you opened them. It's free, it takes five minutes, and it's the most useful thing in your inbox that you actually open on purpose.

- **"Is this free?"**
  The weekly digest is free. Completely, permanently, no-asterisk free. We're building something bigger — a live cohort where you build production web apps with AI, guided by the same thinking that runs through everything on this page. That's coming, and it won't be free. But the digest stands on its own. You don't need to buy anything to get value here.
  *Meta: "See? We told you what we're selling. Most landing pages hide that part."*

- **"Who is this for?"**
  You write code — professionally, seriously, or getting there. You use AI and you're good at it, but sometimes you ship something and you can't quite explain why it works. Or it doesn't work and you can't quite explain why. You know enough to build things but you suspect there are gaps you haven't found yet. You're right. There are. Everyone has them. This is a place where finding them feels like progress, not failure.

- **"Why Claude specifically?"**
  Because it's what we use, and we teach from experience, not theory. Claude is the AI we build with every day. But nothing here is locked to one tool. The thinking skills — knowing what to ask, catching wrong defaults, seeing through surface simplicity — those work whether you're using Claude, Cursor, Copilot, or whatever ships next Tuesday. We teach with Claude. You'll carry it everywhere.

- **"Who makes this?"**
  Eric. He studied how people think, then how computers think, then how to explain one to the other. A Master's in Education made it official. But classrooms move in semesters and tech moves in weeks, so he became a developer and found his way back to teaching on his own terms.
  **How hasToggle started** (expandable):
  He spent three years as a junior dev shipping internal tools on a server-side JavaScript engine from 2007 with no modern syntax. Then went independent as a senior engineer and lead web dev coach, mentoring bootcamp cohorts from "what's a variable" to shipping production Next.js apps. When over a hundred students kept struggling with React state, he didn't explain it again — he built an interactive tool that shows what actually happens under the hood.
  That tool was the first hasToggle demo. The rest followed from the same instinct: if the explanation isn't landing, build something that makes it impossible to misunderstand.
  *Meta: "He also hacked his dorm's laundry machines for two years. Some people question systems professionally. Some start early."*

### 6. Footer

Tagline echo: "You just scrolled past four misconceptions and a fake statistic. Welcome to hasToggle."

## Key Design Principles

1. **Misconceptions are the structural principle** — not a section, not a gimmick. Every piece of content surfaces a specific gap in understanding.
2. **Meta-commentary is the voice** — the site's self-awareness is the brand personality. It's structural (option C), not decorative.
3. **Three layers always** — surface, meta, demo. The obvious reading is never the whole story.
4. **Selective meta moments** — one in four, not every line. The wit lands because it's earned, not sprayed.
5. **Positive energy in messaging, confrontational energy in demos** — the page inspires, the demos challenge.
6. **The page teaches by existing** — it looks like a landing page, but it's already demonstrating the skill hasToggle builds: looking past the surface.
7. **Code is the proving ground, not the boundary** — hasToggle starts with web development because that's where misconceptions are concrete and demos are interactive. But the thinking skills scale beyond code. The product should evolve as AI does.

# hasToggle Landing Page — Positioning & Messaging Design

## Core Positioning

**Tagline**: "AI makes you more."

**Subline**: "For developers who want to think sharper, not just ship faster."

**Core belief** (subliminal, carried through content over time):
AI doesn't replace you. It reveals you. It reveals what you understand, what you assumed, what you never questioned. And when you learn to use it well, it amplifies everything you are. The future belongs to people who use AI to become more — not less — human.

**Thesis**: AI is getting smarter fast. It already writes code, it's learning to reason, and it's showing early signs of meta-cognition — generating solutions that test designers didn't anticipate. Within a year or two, thinking in meta-levels won't be a human advantage; it'll be an AI default. When that happens, the bottleneck shifts again: not "can you think clearly about code" but "can you navigate a world where AI thinks clearly too?" hasToggle starts where the pain is today — helping developers build better software with AI. But the mission is larger: teaching people to understand AI, work alongside it, and make good decisions in a world that's changing faster than any institution can prepare them for. Code is the proving ground. The skill is universal.

## Audience

**Primary (launch)**: Working developers who want to level up. They use AI daily but keep hitting walls they can't explain. They can get code out of Claude, but they can't always tell if it's right.

**Vision (long-term)**: Anyone who needs to navigate AI effectively — not just developers. As AI reaches AGI-level capabilities, understanding how to work with it, question it, and direct it becomes a universal skill. hasToggle starts with code because that's where the feedback loop is tightest: you build something, it works or it doesn't, you learn why. But the meta-skills — questioning assumptions, productive uncertainty, knowing what to ask — transfer to every domain AI touches. Which will be all of them.

## Competitive Positioning

**Relationship to AI Hero / Claude Code for Real Engineers**: Complementary, not competitive. Different layers entirely. AI Hero teaches how to operate Claude Code professionally. hasToggle teaches the thinking underneath — the judgment that makes any tool effective. Someone could and should use both.

**Relationship to Claude**: Claude is the primary AI tool taught with. Content is built around working with Claude. But the meta-skills transfer to any AI. Claude is the vehicle, not the destination.

## Business Model

Free content (weekly digest, misconception demos) builds the audience. Paid live cohort in a similar price range to AI Hero (~$795) is the product. Gain traction first, monetize second.

## Voice Architecture

The page operates on three layers. All three teach the same lesson: the obvious reading is never the whole story.

### Layer 1: Surface

Clean, professional, aspirational copy. Works standalone for people who don't hover or dig.

- **Tone**: Confident, positive, forward-looking
- **Example**: "AI makes you more."

### Layer 2: Meta

Self-aware site commentary delivered through tooltips, footnotes, badges, and asides. This is where the brand personality lives. The site knows it's a landing page and comments on its own structure.

- **Tone**: Pratchett wit — honest, clever, self-referential. Fourth-wall breaking in the style of Deadpool or PostHog's self-aware marketing copy.
- **Character**: The site itself. Not Eric, not a mascot. The page is self-aware.
- **Example**: Tooltip on tagline: "It sounds wrong until you think about it."
- **Distribution**: Selective, not constant. One in four moments, not every line. Pratchett didn't footnote every sentence.

### Layer 3: Demo

Interactive misconception reveals. The most confrontational layer, but earned by the other two.

- **Tone**: "You thought this was fine. Here's why it's not. Now you know."
- **Structure**: Hook (the misconception) → meta aside → interactive proof → reality statement → side story (expandable)
- **Side stories**: Expandable asides that connect the coding misconception to a broader real-world analogy. Click to open, self-contained. This is where the "bigger than code" vision lives — proving the thinking skill transfers beyond software. Not every demo needs one, but the best ones will have them.

### Tone Duality

- **Messaging and positioning**: Aspirational, positive, empowering. "You're becoming the engineer this era needs."
- **Demos and exercises**: Confrontational reveals. "You thought this was fine. Here's why it's not."
- The demos earn the trust that the positive messaging promises.

## Page Structure

The page is organized around misconception reveals, not traditional marketing sections. Each section has surface copy, meta-commentary, and where possible, an interactive demo. The page itself is a misconception demo — it looks like a landing page, but it's already teaching.

### 1. Hero

- **Surface**: Tagline + one-liner + CTA
- **Meta**: Tooltip on tagline; fourth-wall break on CTA
- **No demo** — the hero earns attention, it doesn't prove anything yet

**Draft copy:**

> **AI is never uncertain. That's why you have to be.**
>
> hasToggle teaches working developers the thinking skills that make AI useful — not just fast. One misconception at a time.

- CTA: "Get the weekly misconception buster"
- Meta under CTA: "This is the email capture. You knew it was coming."

### 2. Misconception Demos (the core of the page)

Multiple demos, each following the same structure: hook → meta aside → interactive demo → reality statement. These are the page. Not a section — the structural spine.

**Demo 1 — React State:**

- Hook: "AI writes the code so I don't need to understand it."
- Meta: "86% of developers have shipped code they couldn't explain. We made that number up. But you believed it for a second."
- Demo: Counter showing React re-renders
- Reality: "AI helps you type code, but you still need to understand how things work."

**Demo 2 — Destructive Defaults:**

- Hook: "I told AI exactly what I wanted."

- Demo: System that handles errors by deleting records. AI followed every instruction. None were questioned.
- Reality: "AI builds what you ask for. Not what you need. The gap between those is your job."
- Did you know? **"The safest brake is the one that's always on."**
  Heavy trucks use air brakes. This is interesting for a reason that has nothing to do with trucks and everything to do with the demo you just saw.
  In a car, you press the brake pedal and fluid pushes the pads against the wheels. If the fluid leaks out, you have no brakes. This is what engineers call a "failure mode," and what passengers call "screaming."
  Truck engineers, being the sort of people who think about what happens when things go wrong* — looked at this and made a decision that seems obvious in hindsight and brilliant in foresight: they reversed it. In a truck, massive springs hold the brakes *on* by default. Air pressure is what *releases* them. If a line is cut, if the compressor dies, if anything fails at all, the springs do what springs do and the truck stops.
  The safe state isn't something the system has to achieve. It's where the system already is.
  Now look at the demo above. A developer asked AI to "handle errors." AI handled them. By deleting the records. Nobody asked "what should the *default* be when something goes wrong?" — and so the default became the worst possible thing, delivered with the quiet confidence of a system that is working exactly as instructed.
  The truck engineer and the software developer faced the same question. One of them thought about it first.**

  *\*\*The other one shipped to production on a Friday.*

**Demo 3 — Optimistic Updates:**

  **The user sees:**
  Nothing happened. *Clicks again.* Two items in cart. *Refreshes.* Zero items in cart.

  (How perspectives manifest in UI and how the dev view displays stack slices — to be designed in detail later.)

- Reality: "A button is a vertical slice through your entire application. It touches the client, the server, the database, the auth layer, and the user's trust. The only simple thing about it is how it looks."

### 3. About / Values

- **Surface**: Three core values, each with a short explanation:

  **AI makes you more, not less.**
  AI is the most powerful tool individual developers have ever had. It handles cognitive load so you can focus on what actually matters — architecture, product decisions, and shipping. You're not being replaced. You're being extended.

  AI is getting better at reasoning, but it can't check your assumptions for you. The skill that matters most is meta-cognition: questioning your own premises, noticing what you didn't consider, knowing when to stop and ask "is this actually right?"

  *\*See Demo 4. We warned you.*

- **Meta** on the section: "Most About sections exist so the founder can talk about themselves. This one exists because we thought you'd want to know what you're getting into."

### 4. Digest CTA

- **Surface**: "Every Monday, we prove you wrong about something." + email signup
- **Meta** near email input: "The fact that you're reading the fine print under an email form says something about you. Something good."

### 5. FAQ

Mostly straight answers. Meta moments on select items.

- **"Does AI really get things wrong?"**
  It does. Frequently. With absolute confidence. That's exactly why you need to understand the fundamentals. AI is powerful but not infallible. We teach you to review, debug, and guide AI so you catch mistakes before they ship. Understanding how code works is what separates builders from prompt-typists.
  *Meta: "It does. Frequently. With absolute confidence. That's lesson one."*

- **"What do I actually get?"**

- **"Who is this for?"**
